revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Glossary of Shinto]][[es:Anexo:Glosario de sintoísmo]][[ja:神道用語一覧]][[simple:Glossary of Shinto]][[uk:Словник синто]]
{{cleanup|date=July 2022|reason=Many problematic entries: links to non-existent Wiktionary pages, mistaken romanization, misspellings of the Japanese, incorrect glosses}} {{Short description|none}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2022}}{{Dynamic list}} This is the '''glossary of [[Shinto]]''', including major terms on the subject. Words followed by an asterisk (*) are illustrated by an image in one of the photo galleries. __NOTOC__ {{compact ToC|side=yes|top=yes|num=yes}} {{Shinto}} ==A== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|red cow}}||{{ill|Akabeko|en|Akabeko|qid=Q1660457|lt=Akabeko|1=Akabeko}}}}''' – A red {{ill|Papier-mâché|en|Papier-mâché|qid=Q899363|lt=Papier-mâché|1=Papier-mâché}} cow {{ill|Bobblehead|en|Bobblehead|qid=Q359678|lt=Bobblehead|1=Bobblehead}} toy; a kind of ''engimono'' and an ''{{ill|Omiyage|en|Omiyage|qid=Q63224833|lt=Omiyage|1=Omiyage}}'' (a regional souvenir in Japan) that is considered symbolic of {{ill|Aizu|en|Aizu|qid=Q1205071|lt=Aizu|1=Aizu}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|chemise fan}}||Akomeôgi}}''' – A type of fan held by aristocratic women of the Heian period when formally dressed; it is brightly painted with [[:en:Tassels|Tassels]] and streamers on the ends. Held today in Shinto by a ''miko'' in formal costume for festivals. See also ''hiôgi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|evil}}||Aku}}''' – The term's meaning is not limited to moral evil, and includes misfortune, inferiority and unhappiness.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|devil}}||[[Akuma (folklore)|Akuma]]}}''' – A malevolent fire spirit, demon or devil. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|evil tower}}||Akuru}}''' – Also known as the ''Akujin'', the ''Kibi-no-Ananowatari-no-Kami'' and as the ''Anato-no-Kami'', ''Akuru'' is a malevolent ''kami'' that is mentioned in the ''Keikoki'' (records regarding the time of the Emperor Keiko), the ''Nihonshoki'' (Chronicles of Japan), and the ''Kojiki'' (The Records of Ancient Matters). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|evil spirit}}||Akuryō}}''' – A [[:en:Malevolent spirit|Malevolent spirit]], demon or devil. * '''{{nihongo|||Akuryō Taisan}}''' – A spell or command to dispel a malevolent spirit, demon, or devil. One of the earliest uses of this phrase can be seen in ''{{ill|Dōjōji (Noh play)|en|Dōjōji (Noh play)|qid=Q3007824|lt=Dōjōji|1=Dōjōji (Noh play)}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heaven(ly)}}||{{ill|Ama|en|Ama|qid=Q228873|lt=Ama|1=Ama}}}}''' – The divine/deva realm of incarnation, the highest realm on the Wheel of Reincarnation. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heaven(ly) newborn}}||[[Hōko (doll)#Amagatsu|Amagatsu]]}}''' – See ''hōko''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly descent}}| & |Amakudari}}''' – A (living) being who is an incarnation of a divine being; an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|qid=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}}. * '''[[Amano-Iwato]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Heavenly Rock Cave}}) – In Shinto, Amano-Iwato is the name of the {{ill|Cave|en|Cave|qid=Q35509|lt=Cave|1=Cave}} where Amaterasu fled after the violent actions of Susanoo caused the death of one of her weavers. Thus, the land was deprived of light, and ''mononoke'' from hell were free to roam the lands and wreak havoc. It took the other ''kami'' to lure her out again, restoring the sun to the world. * '''[[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Dread Star of Heaven|August Star of Heaven}}), also-known-as {{nihongo|||Ame-no-kagase-o}} – Originally a rebellious Shinto god who would not submit to the will of the other ''[[Amatsukami]]''. Under {{ill|Chinese Buddhism|en|Chinese Buddhism|qid=Q847618|lt=Chinese Buddhist|1=Chinese Buddhism}} influence,<ref>New Larousse encyclopedia of mythology, by Félix Guirand and Robert Graves, Hamlyn, 1968, p.415</ref> the god was identified with {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}}, either as the [[:en:Pole Star|Pole Star]] or {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|qid=Q313|lt=Venus|1=Venus}}, before being combined with the god of all stars, {{nihongo3|{{lit|Divine Lord of the Middle-Heavens}}||Ama-no-mi-naka-nushi}}. In some versions, Amatsu-Mikaboshi was born from the blood of Kagutsuchi spilt by Izanagi, after Kagutsuchi's birth. Amatsu-Mikaboshi is mentioned in passing in the ''Nihon Shoki'' as being subdued by [[Takemikazuchi]], during the latter's conquest of the land of ''{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|malevolent spirit from heaven}}||{{ill|Amanojaku|en|Amanojaku|qid=Q2417460|lt=Amanojaku|1=Amanojaku}}}}''' – An ''oni''-like creature in Japanese folklore; the ''amanojaku'' is thought to be able to provoke a person's darkest desires, and can instigate them into perpetrating wicked deeds. Similar to Amanozako. See also ''jaki'' and ''jama''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|floating bridge of heaven}}||[[Takamagahara|Ama-no-uki-hashi]]}}''' – In Shinto, it is the bridge connecting Earth and Takamagahara. * '''{{ill|Amanozako|en|Amanozako|qid=Q978414|lt=Amanozako|1=Amanozako}}''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Opposing Heaven}}) – A goddess mentioned in the ''[[Kujiki]]'', which states that she originated when Susanoo let his ''ara-mitama'' build up inside him until he vomited her out. Similar to ''amanojaku''. * '''[[Amaterasu]] Ōmikami''' ({{lang|ja|, or }}, {{lit|Heaven(ly) Illumination Great Honourable Imperial Divinity}}) – The Shinto sun goddess, tutelary ''kami'' and ancestor of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}, enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref name="bocking"/> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Heavenly Deities}}||[[Amatsukami]]}}''' – ''Kami'' from Takamagahara. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Crimes in Heaven}}| / |Amatsu tsumi}}''' – A term for tsumi specifically committed against heaven. For example, the crimes committed by Susanoo-no-Mikoto against Amaterasu are considered ''amatsu tsumi''. The corresponding concept to ''amatsu tsumi'' is ''kunitsu tsumi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Jewel(ed) Spear of Heaven}}|, or |[[Ame-no-Nuboko|Ame-no-Nuhoko]]}}''' – The spear used by [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} to raise the primordial landmass, ''Onogoro-shima'', from the sea; it is often depicted as a ''naginata''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Shining Heavenly Sky Goddess}}| or |[[Ame-no-Uzume]]}}''' – The Shinto goddess of the dawn, mirth, meditation, revelry and the arts, and the wife of Sarutahiko Ōkami. See also Otafuku. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|table|platform}}||[[An (Shinto)|An]]*}}''' – A small portable table or platform used during Shinto ceremonies to bear offerings. It may have four, eight or sixteen legs. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|that world}}||Ano-Yo}}''' – See ''seishinkai''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|safety|security}}||Anzen}}''' – A type of ''omamori'', specifically for safety, particularly safety at work, frequently requested from a ''kami'', and in fact corporations often have a {{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=tutelary shrine|1=Chinjusha}} specifically to ensure their business prospers.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[:en:Hollyhock|Hollyhock]] Festival}}||[[Aoi Matsuri]]}}''' – One of the three main annual festivals held in Kyoto, Japan (the other two being the Festival of the Ages (Jidai Matsuri) and the Gion Festival). It is a festival of the two ''kamo'' shrines in the north of the city: Shimogamo Shrine and Kamigamo Shrine. The festival may also be referred to as the ''Kamo'' Festival. It is held on 15 May of each year. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Wild Soul}}||{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Ara-mitama|1=Mitama}}}}''' – The rough and violent side of the ''mitama''.<ref name=ara>{{cite encyclopedia|last=Yonei|first=Teruyoshi|title=Aramitama|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1180|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=5 February 2011}}</ref> The ''ara-mitama'' is associated with the colours black and purple, and the cardinal direction north. ''Ama-no-Zako'' is actually the incarnated ''ara-mitama'' of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|wandering shrine maiden}}||Aruki miko}}''' – An itinerant ''miko'' that is not in service to a particular shrine, and wanders throughout the country performing services where needed, and living off-of charity. * '''{{nihongo3|lit. The "Middle country of {{ill|Reed bed|en|Reed bed|qid=Q620424|lt=Reed bed|1=Reed bed}}s"||[[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]}}''' – In Shinto, this term is applied to the plane of existence that exists between ''Takamagahara'' and ''Yomi'', or the realm of the living. The term became another word for the country or the location of Japan itself. The term can be used interchangeably with ''[[Names of Japan#Classical|Toyoashihara no Nakatsukuni]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[Om]]}}||[[Om|A-un]]}}''' – In ''Shinto''-''Buddhism'', ''a-un'' is the transliteration in Japanese of the two syllables "a" and "hūṃ", written in {{ill|Devanagari|en|Devanagari|qid=Q38592|lt=Devanagari|1=Devanagari}} as {{script|Deva|अहूँ}} (the syllable, ''[[Om]]''). See also ''{{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|qid=Q539715|lt=Nio|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}'' and ''{{ill|Ox-Head and Horse-Face|en|Ox-Head and Horse-Face|qid=Q2184252|lt=Gozu and Mezu|1=Ox-Head and Horse-Face}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Strange, Unusual, Supernatural, Paranormal, Extraordinary}}||{{ill|Ayakashi (yōkai)|en|Ayakashi (yōkai)|qid=Q17005117|lt=Ayakashi|1=Ayakashi (yōkai)}}}}''' – An umbrella term that covers ghosts, phantoms, phantasms, apparitions and illusions, goblins, monsters, demons, devils and any kind of supernatural beasts and beings; the corporeal and the incorporeal; real or fantasy; ''ayakashi'' is a term more specific for ''yōkai'' that appear above the surface of some body of water. See also ''rinka'', {{ill|Shiranui (optical phenomenon)|en|Shiranui (optical phenomenon)|qid=Q1084671|lt=shiranui|1=Shiranui (optical phenomenon)}}, ''{{ill|Will-o'-the-wisp|en|Will-o'-the-wisp|qid=Q160569|lt=Will-o'-the-wisp|1=Will-o'-the-wisp}}, and'' ''{{ill|St. Elmo's fire|en|St. Elmo's fire|qid=Q120688|lt=St. Elmo's fire|1=St. Elmo's fire}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[Betula grossa|cherry birch]] bow}}||[[:en:Azusa yumi|Azusa yumi]]}}''' – a sacred bow used in certain Shinto rituals in Japan, as well as a Japanese musical bow; made from the wood of the Japanese cherry birch tree (''Betula grossa''). Playing an ''azusa yumi'' forms part of some Shinto rituals; in Japan, it is universally believed that merely the twanging of the bowstring will frighten ghosts and evil spirits away from a house.
==B== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|transforming thing}}||[[:en:Bakemono|Bakemono]]}}''' – A monstrous apparition; a monster or ghost. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|ten thousand things}}||Banbutsu}}''' – A term used to refer to the whole world. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|ten thousand likenesses}}||Banshō}}''' – A term describing all things. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|separate {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|qid=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}}/[[shrine]]}}||Bekkū'' or ''betsugū}}''' – A {{ill|Subsidiary|en|Subsidiary|qid=Q658255|lt=Subsidiary|1=Subsidiary}} shrine next to the ''honden'', which may however enshrine an equally important ''kami''.<ref name=bocking /> * '''[[Benzaiten]]''' ({{lang|ja|, }}, {{lit|{{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}ly-[[Happy]] Talents}}) – Originally a Vedic goddess, [[:en:Sarasvati|Sarasvati]], Benzaiten is a {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=syncretic|1=Syncretism}} goddess and a member of the [[Seven Lucky Gods]]. She is also syncretized with {{nihongo3|{{lit|Female [goddess] of the Island of Worship}}||Ichikishima-hime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|acting-division head}}||{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|qid=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}}}''' – Before the shinbutsu bunri, when the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} law forbade the mixing of Shinto and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, a ''bettō'' was a monk who performed Buddhist rites at a Shinto shrine. * '''{{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}''' – Syncretic deity of Buddhist origin part of the Seven Lucky Gods.<ref name=bocking/> A symbol of authority, he protects warriors. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|qid=Q2601788|lt=Votive offering|1=Votive offering}} {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|qid=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}}}}||{{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Bon Matsuri|1=Obon}}}}''' – A festival celebrated around July 15 in order to console the spirits of the dead. In theory a Buddhist festival, but in practice an ancestor and family festival part of Shinto.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Buddhist temple bell}}||{{ill|Bonshō|en|Bonshō|qid=Q8192099|lt=Bonshō|1=Bonshō}}}}''' – Large bells found in Buddhist temples throughout Japan, used to summon the monks to prayer and to demarcate periods of time. Instead of containing a clapper, ''bonshō'' are struck from the outside using either a handheld mallet or a beam suspended on ropes. See also ''suzu'', ''kagura suzu'', ''rin'', and ''dōtaku''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|qid=Q23925162|lt=Bosatsu|1=Bosatsu}}}}''' – A ''{{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}''. Term of Buddhist origin which is often used for deities of mixed Buddhist/Shinto ancestry such as Benzaiten and ''{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}'', ''kami'' like Hachiman, and deified human beings like {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|deceased spirit}}||{{ill|Yūrei|en|Yūrei|qid=Q11050647|lt=Bōrei|1=Yūrei}}}}''' – A term for a ghost; a type of ''yūrei'', but one whose identity (and grudge) is unknown. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divination, scrying, fortune-telling}}||{{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Bokusen|1=Divination}}}}''' – The act of divining; foreseeing or a foretelling of future events. See also ''futomani'' and ''ukehi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|dance hall}}||Buden}}''' – Another word for a ''kaguraden'', a pavilion or stage dedicated to the performing of the ''kagura''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|soul division}}||[[Bunrei]]}}''' – A process of division of a ''kami'' producing two complete copies of the original, one of which is then transferred to a new shrine by a process called ''[[kanjō]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|shrine division}}||[[Shinto shrine#Famous shrines and shrine networks|Bunsha]]}}''' – A shrine that is a part of a network headed by a more famous shrine, from whence its ''kami'' was transferred by ''kanjō''.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|material world}}||[[:en:Physical world|Busshitsukai]]}}''' – The corporeal world.
==C== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Wisdom|en|Wisdom|qid=Q799|lt=Wisdom|1=Wisdom}}, {{ill|Knowledge|en|Knowledge|qid=Q9081|lt=Knowledge|1=Knowledge}}, {{ill|Intelligence|en|Intelligence|qid=Q83500|lt=Intelligence|1=Intelligence}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Chi]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''{{ill|Bushido|en|Bushido|qid=Q183738|lt=Bushido|1=Bushido}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[:en:Thousand|Thousand]] {{ill|Wood|en|Wood|qid=Q287|lt=Wood|1=Wood}}(en beams)}}||{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|qid=Q262282|lt=Chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}*}}''' – Forked decorations common at the ends of the roof of shrines. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|finishing-touch robe}}||Chihara}}''' – A type of ceremonial overcoat with a long white hem worn by a ''Miko'' in certain Shinto ceremonies; similar to a Kannushi's ''Jōe'' over-robes. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|garrison shrine}}|鎮社|{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinja|1=Chinjusha}}}}''' – The tutelary ''kami'' or tutelary shrine of a certain area or {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}; see also ''chinjusha''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|garrison protector shrine}}||{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinjusha|1=Chinjusha}}*}}''' – a small shrine dedicated to the tutelary ''kami'' of an area or building<ref name=bocking/> (see also ''Chinju''). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-year candy}}||[[Shichi-Go-San#Chitose ame|Chitose ame]]}}''' – Long, thin sticks of red-and-white candy—which symbolizes healthy growth and longevity—sold at festivals for children, specifically for ''Shichi-Go-San''. ''Chitose ame'' is given in a bag decorated with a crane and a turtle—which represent long life in Japan. ''Chitose ame'' is wrapped in thin, clear, and edible rice paper film that resembles plastic. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|portable lantern}}||[[Traditional lighting equipment of Japan#Chōchin|Chōchin]]}}''' – Paper lanterns always present at Shinto festivals. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|butterfly}}||[[Butterfly#In mythology and folklore|Chōchō]]}}''' – Butterflies native to Japan and to Japanese culture. The ''chōchō'' is also featured among ''engimono'' (above).It is seen as lucky, especially if seen in pairs; if a symbol contains two butterflies dancing around each other, it is a symbol of marital happiness. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|animal/livestock}}||{{ill|Animals in Buddhism|en|Animals in Buddhism|qid=Q565697|lt=Chikushō|1=Animals in Buddhism}}}}''' – The mortal, animal realm of incarnation, the third-lowest realm on the wheel of reincarnation. See also ''rinne''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hand-washing earthenware basin}}||{{ill|Chōzubachi|en|Chōzubachi|qid=Q2972160|lt=Chōzubachi|1=Chōzubachi}}}}''' – Usually made of stone, a ''chōzubachi'' is a water bowl, is a vessel used to rinse the hands in Japanese temples, shrines and gardens; see also ''Chōzuya''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hand-washing pavillion}}||{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}}}''' – A Shinto water ablution pavilion for a ceremonial purification rite. The pavilion contains a large water-filled basin called a ''chōzu-bachi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|the calming of the spirits}}||Chinkon}}''' – A Shinto ritual performed for converting ''ara-mitama'' into ''nigi-mitama'', quelling maleficent spirits, prevent misfortune and alleviate fear from events and circumstances that could not otherwise be explained; i.e. ''Ara-mitama'' that failed to achieve deification due to lack of sufficient veneration, or who lost their divinity following attrition of worshipers, became ''yōkai''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Calming-of-the Spirits Service|{{ill|Requiem|en|Requiem|qid=Q51616|lt=Requiem|1=Requiem}}}}||Chinkonsei}}''' – A ''Shinto'' ''Matsuri'' (a festival) performed for converting ''ara-mitama'' into ''nigi-mitama'', quelling maleficent spirits, preventing misfortune and alleviating fear from events and circumstances that could not otherwise be explained. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Duty|en|Duty|qid=Q878070|lt=Duty|1=Duty}} and {{ill|Loyalty|en|Loyalty|qid=Q1132131|lt=Loyalty|1=Loyalty}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Chūgi]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''.
==D== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Great Tastes Festival}}||Daijōsai}}''' – A ceremony marking the beginning of an emperor's reign in which he offers first fruits to ancestors, including Amaterasu.<ref name=bocking/> * '''[[Daikokuten]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Great Black Heavens}}) – A syncretic god, part of the Seven Lucky God fusing {{ill|Mahakala|en|Mahakala|qid=Q922824|lt=Mahakala|1=Mahakala}}, and Ōkuninushi.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hell of Great Screaming}}||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Daikyōkan Jigoku]]}}''' – The fifth level of ''Jigoku''; sinners who have committed murder (even the murder of small creatures such as insects), theft, degeneration, drunkenness, and lying are sent here. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hell of Great Burning}}||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Daishonetsu Jigoku]]}}''' – The seventh level of ''Jigoku''. Sinners who have committed murder (even the murder of small creatures such as insects), theft, degeneration, drunkenness, lying, blasphemy, and rape are sent here. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Den-den daiko|Den-den daiko]]}}''' – a Japanese hand-held {{ill|Pellet drum|en|Pellet drum|qid=Q3514744|lt=Pellet drum|1=Pellet drum}} that is used in Shinto-Buddhist ceremonies. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|travelling guardian divinities}}||{{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|qid=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}}}''' – A group of {{ill|Liminal deity|en|Liminal deity|qid=Q17064682|lt=liminal|1=Liminal deity}} ''kami'' and Buddhist gods, protectors of roads, borders, boundaries and other places of transition.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''Dojin''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|earth god}}) – Another name for any Shinto earth deity. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|copper bell}}||{{ill|Dōtaku|en|Dōtaku|qid=Q2312868|lt=Dōtaku|1=Dōtaku}}}}''' – Large Japanese bronze bells smelted from relatively thin bronze and richly decorated. See also ''bonshō'', ''rin'', and ''suzu''.
==E== * '''{{nihongo||, , , |{{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}}}''' – The ''kami'' of prosperity found at both temples and shrines. One of the Seven Lucky Gods. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|raven (black) hat}}||Eboshi}}''' – A type of {{ill|Pointed hat|en|Pointed hat|qid=Q7208355|lt=Pointed hat|1=Pointed hat}} originally worn by Heian era aristocrats and samurai. Worn by ''Kannushi'' as formalwear for occasions such as festivals and weddings. See also ''kanmuri'' and ''kazaori eboshi''. * '''{{nihongo|||Eirei}}''' – The spirit or ghost of a warrior/soldier who fell in battle. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|picture horse}}||{{ill|Ema (Shinto)|en|Ema (Shinto)|qid=Q687288|lt=Ema|1=Ema (Shinto)}}*}}''' – Small wooden plaques on which worshippers at shrines, as well as Buddhist temples, write their prayers or wishes. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Shinto#Engimono, Ema, divination, and amulets|Engimono]]}}''' – An {{ill|Umbrella term|en|Umbrella term|qid=Q210588|lt=Umbrella term|1=Umbrella term}} for {{ill|Talisman|en|Talisman|qid=Q44506|lt=Talisman|1=Talisman}}s and [[:en:Good luck charm|Good luck charm]]s such as ''{{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|qid=Q1756788|lt=Omamori|1=Omamori}}'' and ''[[ofuda]]''.
==F== * '''{{nihongo|[[Mount Fuji|''Fuji-san'']]|}}''' – The most famous among Japan's three sacred mountains, the {{nihongo||{{lang|ja|三霊山|{{ill|Three Holy Mountains|en|Three Holy Mountains|qid=Q11086198|lt=Sanreizan|1=Three Holy Mountains}}}}|"Three Spiritual Mountains"}}. Mt. Fuji is inhabited by a ''kami'' called [[Konohanasakuya-hime]].<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||[[Fūjin]]}}''' – The ''kami'' of the winds who is the brother of Raijin; together, they are both said to be two of the many sons and other children of Izanagi and Izanami. * '''{{nihongo||{{lang|ja|復古神道|[[Fukkoshinto|Fukko shintō]]}}|"Restoration Shinto"}}''' – A term synonymous with ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}''. * '''{{nihongo||{{lang|ja|太占|{{ill|Futomani|en|Futomani|qid=Q5510530|lt=Futomani|1=Futomani}}}}}}''' – A traditional Shinto system of divination, similar to the Chinese [[oracle bone]] technique, that uses stag bones.
==G== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|elegant music}}||[[Gagaku]]}}''' – Ancient court music that was introduced into Japan with Buddhism from the Korean peninsula and China; now played for Shinto and Buddhist rituals and ceremonies. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hungry ghost}}||{{ill|Preta|en|Preta|qid=Q1332306|lt=Gaki|1=Preta}}}}''' – The "hungry ghost" realm of incarnation, the second-lowest realm on the wheel of reincarnation. See ''rinne''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|outer path box}}||{{ill|Gehōbako|en|Gehōbako|qid=Q123399072|lt=Gehōbako|1=Gehōbako}}}}''' – A supernatural box that is used to hold Shinto paraphernalia, particular to a given ''jinja''; such contents include dolls, animal and human skulls, and Shinto rosaries/prayer beads. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Righteousness|en|Righteousness|qid=Q1144825|lt=Righteousness|1=Righteousness}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Gi]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''''{{ill|Gogyo|en|Gogyo|qid=Q17503617|lt=Gogyo|1=Gogyo}}''''' (五元) – The Five Elements. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Gion Matsuri]]}}''' – One of the three main annual festivals held in Kyoto, Japan (the other two being the ''Aoi Matsuri'' and the ''Jidai Festival''). Gion Matsuri is one of the largest festivals in Japan for purification and pacification of disease-causing-entities. It takes place on 17 and 24 July. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Duty|en|Duty|qid=Q878070|lt=Duty|1=Duty}}}}||{{ill|Giri (Japanese)|en|Giri (Japanese)|qid=Q2661081|lt=Giri|1=Giri (Japanese)}}}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|great {{ill|Wand|en|Wand|qid=Q148493|lt=Wand|1=magic wand}}}}||[[Gohei|Go-hei]]*}}''' – A wooden wand decorated with two ''shide'' and used in Shinto rituals as a ''yorishiro''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|background light}}||Gokō}}''' – The Shinto-Buddhist equivalent of an {{ill|Aureola|en|Aureola|qid=Q374192|lt=Aureola|1=Aureola}} or {{ill|Halo (religious iconography)|en|Halo (religious iconography)|qid=Q3341893|lt=halo|1=Halo (religious iconography)}}. See also ''tenne''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|paradise|pure land}}||Gokuraku Jōdo}}''' – See ''jōdo''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Current {{ill|Authority|en|Authority|qid=Q174834|lt=Authority|1=Authority}}; {{ill|Incarnation|en|Incarnation|qid=Q41374|lt=Incarnation|1=Incarnation}}}}||[[Gongen]]}}''' – A Buddhist god that chooses to appear as a Japanese ''kami'' to take the Japanese to spiritual salvation, and a name sometimes used for shrines (e.g. "Tokusō Gongen") before the ''shinbutsu bunri''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Gongen#Gongen-zukuri|Gongen-zukuri]]}}''' – A shrine structure in which the [[haiden (Shinto)|''haiden'']], the ''heiden'' and the ''[[honden]]'' are connected under the same roof in the shape of an H.* See also ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|honourable soul}}||[[Goryō]]}}''' – A soul, angry for having died violently or unhappy, which needs to be pacified through Buddhist rites or enshrinement, like {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}};<ref name="bocking" /> vengeful Japanese ghosts from the aristocratic classes, especially those who have been martyred. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|five seasonal festivals}}||[[Gosekku]]}}''' – The five annual cultural festivals that were traditionally held in the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Japanese imperial court]]. The ''Gosekku'' were originally adapted from Chinese practices and first celebrated in Japan in the ''Nara'' period in the 8th–10th centuries CE. The festivals were held until the beginning of the Meiji era. * '''{{nihongo|||Gozu-tennō}}''' – Buddhist name of Susanoo, considered an avatar of ''{{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}''.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''[[Ox-Head and Horse-Face#Japanese mythology|Gozu and Mezu]]''' ({{lang|ja|, {{lit|Ox-head}}, and }}, {{lit|Horse-head}}) – In Shinto-Buddhism, Gozu and Mezu are the Japanese names for Niútóu and Mǎmiàn, two guardians of the underworld in Chinese and Shinto-Buddhist mythology. Both have the bodies of men, but Gozu has the head of an ox while Mezu has the face of a horse. They are the first beings a dead soul encounters upon entering ''Jigoku''; in many stories they directly escort the newly dead to the underworld. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|shrine, temple}}||-gū}}''' – A suffix of some shrine names indicating it enshrines a member of the imperial family.<ref name="bocking" /> [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman-gū shrines]], for instance, enshrine {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|army positioning}}||{{ill|Gunbai|en|Gunbai|qid=Q1188570|lt=Gunbai|1=Gunbai}}}}''' – An item associated with leadership and ceremonial significance, back in Ancient Japan; wielded by royalty, aristocracy, ''[[daimyo]]'', military leaders, and ''kannushi''; nowadays used by umpires in sumo.
==H== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Eight Banners Divinity}}||[[Hachiman]]}}''' – A syncretic tutelary ''kami'' of the warrior class. First enshrined at [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]], it consists of three separate figures: {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, his mother, and his wife Himegami.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hachiman architecture}}||{{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|en|Hachiman-zukuri|qid=Q3125455|lt=Hachiman-zukuri|1=Hachiman-zukuri}}}}''' – Shinto architectural style in which two parallel structures with gabled roofs are connecting on the non-gabled side forming a single building which, when seen from the side, gives the impression of two.<ref name="jaha">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/h/hachimanzukuri.htm Hachiman-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref> * '''''{{ill|Tennin|en|Tennin|qid=Q7700291|lt=Hagoromo|1=Tennin}}''''' (, {{lit|Feather[ed] Raiment}}) – The {{ill|Stole (vestment)|en|Stole (vestment)|qid=Q732034|lt=stole-like|1=Stole (vestment)}}, feathered, heavenly {{ill|Kimono|en|Kimono|qid=Q483444|lt=Kimono|1=Kimono}} or mantle of ''{{ill|Tennin|en|Tennin|qid=Q7700291|lt=Tennin|1=Tennin}}'' (see below), spiritual beings found in Japanese ''Shinto-Buddhism''; ''hagoromo'' allowed the ''tennin'' wearing them to fly. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hall of Prayer}}||[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]}}''' – A shrine building dedicated to prayer, and the only one of a shrine open to [[laity]]. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hakama|en|Hakama|qid=Q164668|lt=Hakama|1=Hakama}}}}''' – A type of traditional Japanese clothing; originally inspired from ''kù'' ({{Lang-zh|s=裤|t=褲}}), trousers used by the Chinese imperial court in the Sui and Tang dynasties. This style was adopted by the Japanese in the form of the ''hakama'', beginning in the sixth century. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hun and po|en|Hun and po|qid=Q1059771|lt=Haku|1=Hun and po}}}}''' – Stemming from [[Taoism]], ''haku'' is the complement of ''kon'', and is the {{ill|Vitalism|en|Vitalism|qid=Q191631|lt=life force|1=Vitalism}} that is attached to the body, and returns to the earth after death. * '''{{nihongo|||Hakurei}}''' – The soul or spirit of a person who has usually just died. * '''{{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|qid=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan|1=Mount Haku}}''' – Collective name given to three mountains worshiped as ''kami'' and sacred to the Shugendō.<ref>{{EOS|Hakusan Shinkō|1082|Nogami, Takahiro|August 8, 2011}}</ref> Hakusan shrines are common all over Japan. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|evil-banishing arrow}}||{{ill|Hamaya|en|Hamaya|qid=Q138804|lt=Hamaya|1=Hamaya}}}}''' – Decorative arrows bought for good luck at Shinto shrines at New Year's and kept at home all year.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|evil-banishing bow}}||[[:en:Hama Yumi|Hama Yumi]]}}''' – A sacred bow. * '''{{nihongo|||Han-[[honji suijaku]]}}''' – A theory conceived by Yoshida Kanetomo which reversed the standard ''honji suijaku'' theory, asserting Buddhist gods were [[:en:Avatars|Avatars]] of Japanese ''kami''.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo|||Hanagasa}}''' – A flowered-hat worn by ''miko'' during festivals. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Prajñā (Buddhism)|en|Prajñā (Buddhism)|qid=Q849649|lt=Prajñā|1=Prajñā (Buddhism)}}}}||{{ill|Hannya|en|Hannya|qid=Q1205100|lt=Hannya|1=Hannya}}}}''' – A female ''yōkai'' found in Japanese folklore (and in ''[[kagura]]'' and [[:en:Noh Theater|Noh]]), and is most often described as a monstrous ''oni'' of a female; a ''hannya'' is a ''yōkai'' who was originally once a normal mortal human woman, but one who has become so overcome with her jealousy that it has metaphorically consumed her, followed by literally transforming her. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|purification}}||{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|qid=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}}}''' – General term for rituals of purification in Shinto.<ref name="bocking" /> Methods of purification include ''[[misogi]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|place of purification}}||Haraedo}}''' – A place where ritual purification is performed. * '''{{nihongo|||Haraedo-no-Kami}}''' – ''Kami'' of purification. Amongst the many ''kami'' born when Izanagi performed ''misogi'' in order to cleanse the netherworld filth on him after he had returned from his futile attempt to retrieve his late consort, Izanami. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|purification wand}}||[[:en:Haraegushi|Haraegushi]]}}''' – an ''ōnusa'' having a hexagonal or octagonal wand. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|eight-legged table}}||Hassoku-an}}''' – See ''an''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|first accomplishment}}||[[Hatsumōde]]}}''' – The first shrine visit of the [[Japanese New Year|New Year]]. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|First Rising of the Sun}}||{{ill|First sunrise|en|First sunrise|qid=Q11396515|lt=Hatsuhinode|1=First sunrise}}}}''' – The first sunrise of the New Year. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|first dream}}||[[Hatsuyume]]}}''' – The first dream that a person has in the New Year. In Shinto, it is believed that the subjects of the first dreams of the year are representative of what one's upcoming year will be like. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|fireborn spirit}}||[[Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]]}}''' – A section of a shrine where offerings are presented to the gods. * '''{{nihongo|||Heihaku}}''' – See also ''go-hei''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|vase}}||Heishi}}''' – Small bottles used for holding offerings, such as ''sake''; numbered amongst the ''shingu'' for holding offerings. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine fence}}||[[Himorogi]]}}''' – Temporary sacred spaces or altars used to worship. ''Himorogi'' are usually areas demarcated with green bamboo or {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=Cleyera japonica|1=Cleyera japonica}} at the four corners supporting ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|girls' festival}}||{{ill|Hinamatsuri|en|Hinamatsuri|qid=Q844110|lt=Hinamatsuri|1=Hinamatsuri}}}}''' – A ceremony held on 3 March, celebrating the women of Japan, as well as expressing wishes for their continued good health. Originally celebrated as the "Peach Festival", it became known as ''Hina-Matsuri'' during the reign of {{ill|Empress Meishō|en|Empress Meishō|qid=Q120564|lt=Empress Meishō|1=Empress Meishō}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|balls of fire}}||Hi-no-tama}}''' – Fireballs whose presence indicate supernatural activity. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|cypress fan}}||Hiōgi}}''' – A fan used originally by Heian aristocrats, and today by Shinto priests in formal settings.<ref name="bocking" /> See also ''akomeôgi'' (above) * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|qid=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}}}''' – A style of construction in which the building has its main entrance on the side that runs parallel to the roof's ridge (non gabled-side). The ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'', ''{{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|en|Hachiman-zukuri|qid=Q3125455|lt=Hachiman-zukuri|1=Hachiman-zukuri}}'', and ''[[:en:Hie-zukuri|Hie-zukuri]]'' styles belong to this type. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|flat plate}}||Hirazara}}''' – Numbered amongst the ''shingu'' (tools used in shrine altars and home altars) for holding offerings, specifically one for holding {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}} and one for holding {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|qid=Q11254|lt=Salt|1=Salt}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|human pillar}}||{{ill|Hitobashira|en|Hitobashira|qid=Q2615315|lt=Hitobashira|1=Hitobashira}}}}''' – A human sacrifice, buried alive under or near large-scale buildings like dams, bridges and castles. ''Hitobashira'' can also refer to workers who were buried alive under inhumane conditions. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|human soul}}||{{ill|Hitodama|en|Hitodama|qid=Q1621181|lt=Hitodama|1=Hitodama}}}}''' – The wayward soul of a human being who has already died. * '''''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''''' (, {{lit|monad ''kami''}}) – Shinto ''kami'' who came into being alone, as opposed to those who came into being as male-female pairs. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|money offering}}||Hōhei/hōbei}}''' – Offerings made to a ''kami'', usually consisting in ''heihaku'', but sometimes of jewels, money, weapons or other objects.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Halberd Bell}}||Hokosaki Suzu}}''' – Similar to a ''Kagura Suzu'', a ''Hokosaki Suzu'' is a set of several bells on a short-staff. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|crawling child}}||[[Hōko (doll)|Hōko]]}}''' – A soft-bodied doll given to young women of age and pregnant women in Japan as an amulet to protect both the new mother and the unborn child. * '''{{nihongo|| or |{{ill|Hokora|en|Hokora|qid=Q1442984|lt=Hokora/hokuraman|1=Hokora}}}}''' – An extremely small shrine. One of the earliest words for ''shrine''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kagu-tsuchi|Ho-musubi]]}}''' – Another name for Kagutsuchi. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|main hall}}||[[Honden]]}}''' – Also called ''shinden'' (神殿) ("divine hall"), the ''honden'' is the most sacred building of a shrine, intended for the exclusive use of the enshrined ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Honji suijaku]]}}''' – A theory dominant for centuries in Japan according to which Japanese ''kami'' are local manifestations of Indian gods. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|main shrine}}||Hongū}}''' – Located only within a ''jingū'', the main shrine enshrining the principal ''kami'', as opposed to ''betsugū'', ''sessha'' or ''massha''. The term includes ''haiden'', ''heiden'' and ''honden''.<ref name="bocking" /> See also ''honsha''. * '''{{nihongo|||Honsha}}''' – The main shrine of a shrine complex. It is followed hierarchically by ''sessha'' and ''massha''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Fangxiangshi#Japanese hōsōshi|Hōsōshi]]}}''' – An obsolete term for the role driving away devils at a religious ceremony, or the driver of the hearse carrying the coffin of a deceased emperor, back in ancient Japan; a {{ill|Ritual|en|Ritual|qid=Q189819|lt=Ritual|1=Ritual}} {{ill|Exorcist|en|Exorcist|qid=Q2467532|lt=Exorcist|1=Exorcist}} fulfilling a role in a funeral, called ''tsuina'' (see below). Originally a {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}} Chinese custom, later adopted by the Japanese during the Heian period. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Buddha}}||Hotoke}}''' – A term meaning either ''Buddha'' or ''dead soul''. While Buddhist in origin, the term is used in the second sense by all Japanese religions.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hundred-times stone}}||Hyakudoishi}}''' – Sometimes present as a point of reference for the ''hyakudomairi'' near the entrance of a shrine or Buddhist temple. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hundred-times visits}}||Hyakudomairi}}''' – A worshiper with a special prayer will visit the shrine a hundred times. After praying, they must go at least back to the entrance or around a ''hyakudoishi'' for the next visit to be counted as separate. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Possession}}||{{ill|Spirit possession|en|Spirit possession|qid=Q1782082|lt=Hyōi|1=Spirit possession}}}}''' – Possession, specifically possession by a spirit or a ''kami''.
==Gallery: A to H== <gallery> Image:An_Shinto.jpg|A ''hassoku-an'' Image:Chigi-Katsuogi-DSC1628.jpg|''Chigi'' (forked decorations) Image:ItsukushimaEma7438.jpg|An ''ema'' Image:Hokora-DSC2202.jpg|A ''hokora'' Image:ShintoShrineHyakudoIshiM0872.jpg|A ''hyakudoishi'' Image:Shinto gohei.jpeg|A ''gohei'' Image:Ikukunitama-jinja_haiden.jpg|A ''haiden'' Image:Meiji Shrine Sando and Torii New Year Worship.jpg|Hatsumōde at [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]], [[:en:Shibuya, Tokyo|Shibuya, Tokyo]] </gallery>
==I== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|living evil spirit}}||Ichijama}}''' – A type of curse from [[Okinawa]]; it is a type of ''ikiryō''—-a spirit of a still-living person which leaves the body to haunt its victim. An ''ichijama'' is enacted using a special doll known as an ''ichijama butokii''. See also ''Ushi no Koku Mairi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|living evil spirit image}}||Ichijama Butokii}}''' – A doll used to enact an ''ichijama'' curse. * '''{{nihongo|||Ichikishima-hime-no-mikoto}}''' – See Benzaiten. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|one spirit, four souls}}||Ichirei Shikon}}''' – A {{ill|Philosophy|en|Philosophy|qid=Q5891|lt=Philosophy|1=Philosophy}} within Shinto in which one's soul consists of a whole spirit called {{nihongo|||naohi}} that is connected with the heaven and the ''shikon'': the ''ara-mitama'', ''kushi-mitama'', ''nigi-mitama'', and ''saki-mitama''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Spirit tablet|Ihai]]}}''' – A {{ill|Placard|en|Placard|qid=Q606876|lt=Placard|1=Placard}} used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor. The name of the deity or past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in traditional Chinese culture, the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries where any form of ancestor veneration is practiced. * '''{{nihongo|||Ikan}}''' – A set of official robes worn by aristocrats and court officials of the Heian-era court. Worn today in Shinto by a ''kannushi'' in formal costume for festivals. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Ikiryō|en|Ikiryō|qid=Q2986373|lt=Ikiryō|1=Ikiryō}}}}''' – In Japanese popular belief, {{ill|Folklore|en|Folklore|qid=Q36192|lt=Folklore|1=Folklore}} and fiction, it refers to a disembodied spirit that leaves the body of a person who is still living and subsequently haunts other people or places, sometimes across great distances.. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|abhorrent}}||Imi}}''' – Something to be avoided, particularly to a ceremony.<ref name=bocking /> See also ''kegare'' and ''tsumi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|abhorrent words}}||Imikotoba}}''' – Words to be avoided in certain occasions.<ref name=bocking /> * '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}*|}}''' – The Shinto ''kami'' of fertility, rice, agriculture, foxes, industry, and worldly success. Inari's shrines can be identified by the stone foxes which adorn it. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|consoling spirits festival}}||Ireisai}}''' – A festival to remember and pacify the spirits of war dead that takes place at {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} and other shrines built to the purpose.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''[[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|The Great Forces Divine Palace}}) – A shrine in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]] considered one of the holiest ''Shinto'' sites. * '''{{nihongo||, & |[[Itako]]/Ichiko}}''' – The blind female [[:en:Shamans|Shamans]] from northwest Honshu who act as a link between human beings and ''kami'', echoing what was probably the former role of ''miko'' in Shinto.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||[[Yorishiro#Iwakura|Iwakura]]*}}''' – A rock where a ''kami'' has been invited to descend for worship. See ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}''. * '''{{nihongo|[[Izanagi]]|}}''' – The [[Sibling relationship#marriage|brother-husband]] of Izanami, Izanagi is one of the Japanese creator ''kami''; according to the ''Nihongi'' and ''Kojiki'', he gave birth to Japan,<ref name="bocking" /> and is the father of Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi and Susanoo. * '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}|}}''' – The [[:en:Sister-wife|Sister-wife]] of Izanagi. She is one of the Japanese creator ''kami'', according to the ''Nihongi'' and ''Kojiki'', gave birth to Japan,<ref name="bocking" /> later dying in childbirth with her last child, Kagutsuchi, who burned her alive and sent her to the Underworld, Izanami becomes a ''kami'' of death.
==J== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|malevolent spirits}}||{{ill|Amanojaku|en|Amanojaku|qid=Q2417460|lt=Jaki|1=Amanojaku}}}}''' – An ''oni''-like creature in Japanese folklore, thought to be able to provoke a person's darkest desires. Similar to the ''amanojaku''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|malevolent demons}}||Jama}}''' – A demon or devil of perversity, a hindrance to the practice of purity in Shinto and the practice of enlightenment in Buddhism. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|ground-pacifying ceremony}}||Jichinsai}}''' – A ceremony held by a Shinto priest on a site before the start of construction on the behalf of owners and workers to pacify and appease local spirits.<ref name= bocking/> * '''{{nihongo3|lit. the "festival of the ages"||{{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|qid=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri|1=Jidai Matsuri}}}}''' – One of the three main annual festivals held in Kyoto, Japan (the other two being the Aoi Matsuri and the Gion Festival). It is held on October 22 every year. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|earth prison}}||{{ill|Naraka (Buddhism)|en|Naraka (Buddhism)|qid=Q1148173|lt=Jigoku|1=Naraka (Buddhism)}}}}''' – The Shinto-Buddhist version of {{ill|Hell|en|Hell|qid=Q564|lt=Hell|1=Hell}} (the Japanese name for ''{{ill|Diyu|en|Diyu|qid=Q1899406|lt=Diyu|1=Diyu}}''). Similar to the [[Dante's Inferno#Nine circles of Hell|Nine Rings of Hell]] in ''[[:en:Dante's Inferno|Dante's Inferno]]'', ''Jigoku'' has eight levels. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|human-eating ghost}}||{{ill|Jikininki|en|Jikininki|qid=Q2756347|lt=Jikininki|1=Jikininki}}}}''' – In Japanese Buddhism, ''jikininki'' are the spirits of greedy, selfish, or impious individuals who are cursed after death to seek out and eat human corpses. See also ''gaki''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|benevolence}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Jin]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Divine Land Spirits Governmental Office}}||[[Department of Divinities]]}}''' – In the ''[[ritsuryō]]'' system, the part of government responsible for festivals.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Divine Palace/Shrine}}||Jingū}}''' – A shrine enshrining a member of the Imperial family, like [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Jingū]], which enshrines the deified spirits of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} and {{ill|Empress Shōken|en|Empress Shōken|qid=Q269520|lt=Empress Shōken|1=Empress Shōken}}. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}}}''' – A temple whose existence is supposed to help the soul of the ''kami'' the shrine next to it enshrines.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Jinja|1=Shinto shrine}}*}}''' – The most general name for a shrine. * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-bukkaku}}''' – Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, especially a combined shrine/temple complex. * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-fukkyū}}''' – A reversal of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}'s ''jinja gappei''. Not to be confused with ''jinja fukushi''.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-fukushi}}''' – A form of unofficial and illegal restoration of a merged shrine. See ''jinja-gappei''.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-gappei}}''' – A policy begun in the early 1900s, when half the shrines were merged with the remainder and disappeared.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||[[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja-honchō]]}}''' – Also known as the Association of Shinto Shrines, it is a group that includes most of the Shinto shrines in Japan.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-kaikan}}''' – A hotel-like building inside large shrines used for weddings. * '''{{nihongo|||Jinja-shinto}}''' – Originally a synonym of State Shinto (Kokka Shinto below), it is now a term criticized by specialists as problematic.<ref name="bocking" /> When applied to post-war Shinto, it means the beliefs and practices associated to shrines, particularly those associated with the Association of Shinto Shrines.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[self-restraint]]}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Jisei]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Jisha|1=Chinjusha}}}}''' – A temple's tutelary shrine. See also ''chinjusha'' (above) and ''jinja-bukkaku''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|pure robes}}||[[Jōe]]}}''' – A formal over-garment worn by ''kannushi'' during religious ceremonies; a silk ''kariginu''. * '''{{nihongo3|"Raising the Roof"|上棟|Jōtō}}''' – A Shinto ceremony held when raising the main beam that forms the ridge of the roof. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|patrol route}}||Junpai}}''' – The custom of visiting a fixed series of 33 or 88 shrines or temples, or shrines-&-temples.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|patrol etiquette}}||[[Junrei]]}}''' – The term most commonly used for either of two major types of {{ill|Pilgrimage|en|Pilgrimage|qid=Q1644573|lt=Pilgrimage|1=Pilgrimage}}s in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, in accordance with Buddhism or Shinto. These pilgrimages can be made as a visit to a group of {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|qid=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}}s, {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}}, or other holy sites, in a particular order, often in a circuit of 33 or 88 sites.
==K== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|turnip arrow}}||{{ill|Kabura-ya|en|Kabura-ya|qid=Q6344481|lt=Kabura-ya|1=Kabura-ya}}}}''' – In Shinto, the sound made by the ''kabura-ya'' in mid-flight is thought to {{ill|Apotropaic magic|en|Apotropaic magic|qid=Q620591|lt=ward-off evil influences|1=Apotropaic magic}}; like the ''hamaya'', the ''kabura-ya'' is used in Shinto cleansing rites of sites, shrine grounds, and parks. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|helmet}}| or |Kabuto}}''' – A helmet—complete with a {{ill|Japanese armour|en|Japanese armour|qid=Q3671179|lt=suit of armour|1=Japanese armour}}—sometimes dedicated to shrines, and indicative of a ''kami's'' power to ward-off and protect from negative influences. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|pine gateposts}}||{{ill|Kadomatsu|en|Kadomatsu|qid=Q2667233|lt=Kadomatsu|1=Kadomatsu}}}}''' – New Year decorations placed in pairs in front of homes to welcome the ''kami'' of the harvest. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|mirror}}||{{ill|Mirror|en|Mirror|qid=Q35197|lt=Kagami|1=Mirror}}}}''' – Often used in Shinto worship; originally [[Bronze mirror#China|bronze mirrors]] were used (see also ''shinkyō''), having been introduced to Japan from China; the most famous example of mirrors in ''Shinto'' is the ''[[Yata no Kagami]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|fire deity}}||[[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagami]]}}''' – Another name for Kagatsuchi. * '''{{ill|Kagome crest|en|Kagome crest|qid=Q16851345|lt=Kagome crest|1=Kagome crest}}''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|lattice eye crest}}) – An {{ill|Apotropaic mark|en|Apotropaic mark|qid=Q27997159|lt=Apotropaic mark|1=Apotropaic mark}}, specifically {{ill|Hexagram|en|Hexagram|qid=Q1164668|lt=Hexagram|1=Hexagram}}s and {{ill|Octagram|en|Octagram|qid=Q2622857|lt=Octagram|1=Octagram}}s featured in Shinto shrines, including the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], to ward off negative and malevolent influences and evil spirits. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine entertainment}}||[[Kagura]]}}''' – A type of Shinto dance with deep ties to the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}} and his family, accompanied by instruments, which is also called ''mikagura'' (御神楽). It is also a type of Shinto dance performed at shrines during religious rites, and is also called ''satokagura'' (里神楽). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine entertainment platform}}||[[Kagura-den]]}}''' – a pavilion or stage dedicated to the performing of the ''kagura''. Also called ''maidono'' or ''buden'' (舞殿). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine entertainment bells}}||{{ill|Kagura suzu|en|Kagura suzu|qid=Q954904|lt=Kagura suzu|1=Kagura suzu}}}}''' – a set of twelve bells used in ''kagura''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Fire worship|en|Fire worship|qid=Q2638573|lt=Kagari-bi|1=Fire worship}}}}''' – {{ill|Candle|en|Candle|qid=Q12888135|lt=Candle|1=Candle}} holders, also called {{nihongo3|{{lit|Candle Holders}}||rōsoku-tate}}. They are designed for burning tiny white candles that are lit whenever one visits the ''kamidana'' for prayers. Some people use little electric lanterns instead of candles. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]]}}''' – The Shinto fire god and patron deity of blacksmiths and ceramic workers. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|forge deity}}||Kajishin}}''' – A divinity of the {{ill|Blacksmith|en|Blacksmith|qid=Q1639825|lt=Blacksmith|1=Blacksmith}}'s {{ill|Forge|en|Forge|qid=Q40558|lt=Forge|1=Forge}}. * '''{{nihongo|||Kakue}}''' – A traditional overcoat-robe worn by Shinto monks. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|concealed form}}||Kakuremi}}''' – A term for a hidden form, invisible form; metaphysical form (of a deity). Also known as ''kakurimi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hidden world}}||Kakuriyo}}''' – Refers to the worlds of ''kami'' and the dead.<ref name="bocking" /> * {{nihongo3|{{lit|kitchen deity}}||[[:en:Kamado-gami|Kamado-gami]]}} a ''kami'' which lives in people's ovens. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|warehouse}}| or カマクラ|Kamakura}}''': ** A winter festival in Akita Prefecture, in mid-January/February, during which children get to play in quinzhee-like snow huts. ** The {{ill|Quinzhee|en|Quinzhee|qid=Q660747|lt=Quinzhee|1=Quinzhee}}-like [[:en:Snow hut|Snow hut]]s made for children during the winter festival of the same-name, in mid-January/February, in Akita Prefecture. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Warehouse Great Light Kami}}|鎌倉大明神|Kamakura Daimyojin}}''': A Shinto ''Kami'' in Akita Prefecture revered during the ''Kamakura'' festival. See also ''[[Suijin]]''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Spirit, God, Deity, Divinity}}||[[Kami]]}}''' – A term broadly meaning ''spirit'' or ''deity'', but has several separate meanings: ** deities mentioned in Japanese mythologies and local deities protecting areas, villages and families.<ref name="smyers">Smyers (1999:219)</ref> ** unnamed and non-anthropomorphic spirits found in natural phenomena.<ref name="smyers" /> ** a general sense of sacred power.<ref name="smyers" /> ** according to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, a ''kami'' is "any thing or phenomenon that produces the emotions of fear and awe, with no distinction between good and evil".{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} * '''{{nihongo|||Kamiarizuki}}''' – A [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] month corresponding roughly to October. Because it is believed that in that month all ''kami'' go to [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], it is called "month with gods" in Izumo.<ref name="bocking" /> See also ''Kannazuki''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Kami Shelf}}||[[Kamidana]]*}}''' – A miniature shrine placed or hung high on a wall in some Japanese homes. * '''''[[:en:Kamikakushi|Kamikakushi]]''''' () – A term used to refer to the mysterious disappearance or death of a person that happens when an angered god takes a person away. Japanese folklore contains numerous tales of humans abducted to the spirit world by ''kami''. See also ''tengu-kakushi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine wind}}||{{ill|Kamikaze (typhoon)|en|Kamikaze (typhoon)|qid=Q1565008|lt=Kamikaze|1=Kamikaze (typhoon)}}}}''' – The two major typhoons that dispersed {{ill|Goryeo under Mongol rule|en|Goryeo under Mongol rule|qid=Q123468225|lt=Mongol-Koryo|1=Goryeo under Mongol rule}} fleets {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=who invaded Japan|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}} under {{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|qid=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan|1=Kublai Khan}} in 1274. * '''{{nihongo|||Kamimukae}}''' – The first part of a typical festival.<ref name="bocking" /> The spirit is usually invited to a ''himorogi''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Kanai Anzen|en|Kanai Anzen|qid=Q3812728|lt=Kanai Anzen|1=Kanai Anzen}}}}''' – A specific kind of ''omamori'' meant to safeguard the safety of one's family. * '''{{nihongo|||Kanayago}}''' – a god of blacksmiths, mainly in Chugoku Region (Shintoism). * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kanjō]]}}''' – A process through which a ''kami'' is transferred to a new shrine. See also ''bunrei''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|qid=Q7800836|lt=Kanju|1=Tide jewels}}}}''' – One of two magical gems that Ryūjin used to control the tides; its counterpart is the ''Manju''. * '''{{nihongo|||Kansan}}''' – A thin jacket for girls of the aristocracy of the Heian period. Now worn by ''miko'' in formal attire for ceremonies and festivals. * '''{{nihongo|||Kanmuri}}''' – A formal traditional [[headdress]] worn by the Japanese emperor and by aristocratic men of the Heian period when formally dressed. Today, it in worn in Shinto by a ''kannushi'' in formal costume for formal ceremonies and festivals. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Kannagara|Kannagara]]}}''' – Another word for Shinto. * '''{{nihongo3|lit. the "Way of the Kami"||Kannagara no Michi}}''' – Another name for Shinto used before World War II.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||Kannazuki}}''' – See also Kamiarizuki. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kannushi]]*}}''' – A Shinto priest who is a master of shrine ceremonies, rituals, and festivals. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hunting robes}}||[[:en:Kariginu|Kariginu]]}}''' – A style of cloak, originally the costume that Heian-era nobles wore when they went out hunting, which became the nobles’ daily casual clothes. Worn today in Shinto by a ''kannushi'' in formal costume for rituals, ceremonies and festivals. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Kasoegi|Kasoegi]]}}''' – See also ''katsuogi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|springtime sun}}||Kasuga }}''' – The tutelary ''kami'' (''[[ujigami]]'') of the entire [[:en:Yamato province|Yamato province]].<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kasuga-zukuri]]}}''' – The architectural style of [[Kasuga-taisha]]. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|blade}}||[[Katana]]}}''' – A {{ill|Sword|en|Sword|qid=Q12791|lt=Sword|1=Sword}}, sometimes dedicated to shrines (along with other weapons, such as ''naginata'' and spears), and indicative of a ''kami's'' power to ward off negative influences. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|model substitute}}||}}''' – A traditional Japanese doll, made of materials like paper or straw, used in certain purification rituals, used as a substitute for a person, as the target for a prayer or curse cast against them. * '''{{nihongo||, , |{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|qid=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}*}}''' – A style of short decorative poles on a shrine's roof set at a right angle to the roof's ridgepole. See also ''kasoegi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[latrine]] deity}}||{{ill|Toilet god|en|Toilet god|qid=Q7813363|lt=Kawaya-no-Kami|1=Toilet god}}}}''' – The ''kami'' of latrines, toilets, [[:en:Water-closet|waterclosets]], and feces. * '''{{nihongo|||Kazaori Eboshi}}''' – A type of {{ill|Pointed hat|en|Pointed hat|qid=Q7208355|lt=Pointed hat|1=Pointed hat}} originally worn by Heian-era aristocrats and samurai. It is now worn by ''kannushi'' as formalwear for occasions such as festivals and weddings. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Fastened-off World}}||[[Kekkai (Buddhism)|Kekkai]]}}''' – a restriction in access to a specific area, barred-off often for the purposes of training &/or purification in esoteric Buddhism (and Hinduism). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|visible world}}||Kenkai}}''' – The world that one can see without any kind of supernatural gift; the world of the living. * '''{{nihongo|||Kenkon}}''' – A term used to refer to the union of opposites. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|household ancillary/domestic deities}}||Kenzokushin}}''' – A type of lesser ''kami'' or ''yōkai'' who serves a higher-Ranked ''kami'' or ''yōkai''. * '''{{nihongo|| or |{{ill|Qi|en|Qi|qid=Q202742|lt=Ki|1=Qi}}}}''' – A vital force forming part of any living entity. See also ''haku''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Kijo (folklore)|en|Kijo (folklore)|qid=Q6142096|lt=Kijo|1=Kijo (folklore)}}}}''' – An ''oni'' woman from Japanese legends. See also ''onibabā'' (below) and ''yama-uba'' (below). * '''{{nihongo|||[[Oni#Demon gate|Kimon]]}}''' – Based on the [[Earthly Branches#Directions|assignment of the twelve zodiac animals to the cardinal directions]], the direction of northeast, known as the direction of {{nihongo3|{{lit|Ox-Tiger}}||ushi-tora}}, is also-known-as the direction of ''Kimon''; one theory is that the ''oni's'' cowlike horns and tigerskin loincloth developed as a visual depiction of this term. According to Taoism and esoteric ''onmyōdō'', the northeasterly direction is considered an unlucky direction through which evil spirits pass, and, as-such, is termed as ''Kimon''; having to travel in this direction was seen as a bad omen for a journey. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|impurity}}||[[Kegare]]}}''' – defilement due to natural phenomena, for example the contact with dead bodies. The opposite of ''kegare'' is ''kiyomi''. See also ''imi'' (above) and ''tsumi'' (below). * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kibitsu-zukuri]]}}''' – The architectural style of [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[:en:Okayama prefecture|Okayama prefecture]], characterized by a huge ''honden'' divided in three parts with an interior painted in vermilion, black and gold.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|||Kigen}}''' – A ritual (i.e. during ''shihobarai'') of banishing evil spirits and other negative influences, which can be achieved by the strumming of bows, such as a ''hama yumi'' and an ''azusa yumi''. * '''{{nihongo|[[Qilin|Kirin]]|}}''' – A mythical creature in Asian mythology; in Shinto, the kirin are considered messengers of the ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Auspicious Heavens}}||[[Kisshōten]]}}''' – A Japanese goddess of good fortune, {{ill|Wealth|en|Wealth|qid=Q200535|lt=Wealth|1=Wealth}} and {{ill|Prosperity|en|Prosperity|qid=Q1760011|lt=Prosperity|1=Prosperity}}. Adapted, via Buddhism, from the Hindu goddess {{ill|Lakshmi|en|Lakshmi|qid=Q131443|lt=Lakshmi|1=Lakshmi}}. Kisshōten is sometimes named as one of the Seven Gods of Fortune, replacing either Jurōjin or Fukurokuju. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|fox}}||{{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|qid=Q692111|lt=Kitsune|1=Kitsune}}*}}''' – Animal believed to have magical powers and to be a messenger to Inari. Inari shrines are always protected by statues of foxes, sometimes wearing red votive bibs. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|fox fire}}||[[Kitsunebi]]}}''' – The {{ill|Atmospheric ghost lights|en|Atmospheric ghost lights|qid=Q8786340|lt=Atmospheric ghost lights|1=Atmospheric ghost lights}} mentioned in legends all across Japan outside {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|fox wedding}}||{{ill|Kitsune no yomeiri|en|Kitsune no yomeiri|qid=Q11571371|lt=Kitsune no yomeiri|1=Kitsune no yomeiri}}}}''' – The ''kitsune no yomeiri'' can refer to several things: [[:en:Atmospheric ghost light|Atmospheric ghost light]]s; a sunshower; and various strange wedding processions that can be seen in classical Japanese ''{{ill|Kaidan|en|Kaidan|qid=Q1341659|lt=Kaidan|1=Kaidan}}'', essays, and legends. The ''kitsune-no-yomeiri'' is always closely related to foxes. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|purity}}||Kiyome}}''' – The concept of purity within Shinto, the state in which all beings start-out as at birth, and can return to again by undergoing acts of ''harae'', such as ''misogi''. The opposite of ''kiyomi'' is ''kegare''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Filial piety|en|Filial piety|qid=Q614263|lt=Filial piety|1=Filial piety}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of ''Bushidō'' (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Kō]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|lecture}}||Kō}}''' – A lay worship group focusing on a particular ''kami'' or sacred location which may perform pilgrimages and other rites. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|small ingot}}||{{ill|Koban (coin)|en|Koban (coin)|qid=Q43940|lt=Koban|1=Koban (coin)}}}}''' – Originally currency in the Edo period. In modern times, they are sold as ''engimono'' from Shinto shrines, or, at-least, symbolically, gold-foil cardboard versions are given in the place of the solid-gold ingots, particularly as decorations for a ''kumade'', both attained from Shinto shrines for good fortune in business. * '''{{nihongo3|lit. the "Imperial Way"||Kōdō}}''' – Shinto as defined by post-Meiji nationalist.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo|[[Kogakkan University]]|}}''' – A university located near Ise Shrine that is one of two universities authorized to train Shinto priests. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Records of Ancient Matters|An Account of Ancient Matters}}||[[Kojiki]]}}''' – An early Japanese written chronicle of myths, legends, songs, genealogies, oral traditions, and semi-historical accounts down to 711–712 AD. Similar to the ''Nihon Shoki''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|State Shinto}}||Kokka Shinto}}''' – Japanese translation of the English term ''State Shinto'' created in 1945 by the US occupation forces to define the post-Meiji religious system in Japan. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heart}}||Kokoro}}''' – The essence of a thing or being. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|qid=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin Daigaku|1=Kokugakuin University}}}}''' – Tokyo university that is one of two authorized to train Shinto priests. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|hell of black rope}}||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Kokujō Jigoku]]}}''' – The second level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners,who have committed murder and theft are . * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|qid=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}}*}}''' – Stone warden {{ill|Mane (lion)|en|Mane (lion)|qid=Q59651999|lt=maned|1=Mane (lion)}}-dogs usually present at the entrance of a shrine. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|soul}}||{{ill|Hun and po|en|Hun and po|qid=Q1059771|lt=Kon|1=Hun and po}}}}''' – Stemming from Taoism, ''kon'' is the part of the soul that goes to heaven and is able to leave the body, carrying with it an appearance of physical form; the subliminal self. See also ''tamashii''. * '''{{nihongo|||Konaoshi}}''' – Attire of the ''Heian'' court, now worn by ''kannushi'' in formal functions. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|qid=Q695709|lt=Kongōsho|1=Vajra}}}}''' – A ritual weapon, resembling a ''{{ill|Yawara|en|Yawara|qid=Q1698640|lt=Yawara|1=Yawara}}'' and a ''{{ill|Vajra-mushti|en|Vajra-mushti|qid=Q7908937|lt=Vajra-mushti|1=Vajra-mushti}}''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Konjin|en|Konjin|qid=Q6429460|lt=Konjin|1=Konjin}}}}''' – An itinerant ''kami'' from ''onmyōdō''. Konjin is associated with compass directions, and said to change position with the year, lunar month, and season. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|this world}}||Kono-yo}}''' – See ''busshitsukai''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hun and po|en|Hun and po|qid=Q1059771|lt=Konpaku|1=Hun and po}}}}''' – Every living human has both a ''kon'' and a ''haku''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Rice}}||[[Rice#Cultural roles of rice|Kome]]}}''' – Offerings of white rice made at Shinto shrines and a household's ''kamidana''. See also ''ō-kome''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Kotoamatsukami]]}}''' – The collective name for the first ''Kami'' which came into existence at the time of the creation of the universe, according to the ''Kokiji''. They were came into being up in Takamagahara at the time of creation. Unlike later gods, these deities were born without any procreation. See also ''Amatsukami'' (above). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|channelling miko}}||Kuchiyose miko}}''' – A ''miko'' acting as a {{ill|Mediumship|en|Mediumship|qid=Q25416928|lt=spirit medium|1=Mediumship}}. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|qid=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}}}}''' – A Shinto ''kami'' of local knowledge and agriculture, represented in Japanese mythology as a {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|qid=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}}, who cannot walk but has comprehensive self-awareness and {{ill|Omniscience|en|Omniscience|qid=Q1424662|lt=Omniscience|1=Omniscience}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Nine Hand Seals}}||[[Kuji-in#Meanings in Shinto|Kuji-in]]}}''' – A system of ''{{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}}'' and associated ''{{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}}'' that consist of nine syllables. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Nine Symbolic Cuts}}||{{ill|Kuji-kiri|en|Kuji-kiri|qid=Q1014383|lt=Kuji-kiri|1=Kuji-kiri}}}}''' – A system of ''mudras'' and associated ''mantras'' that consist of nine syllables, based on ''Kuji-in''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|bear hand}}||Kumade}}''' – A [[Rake (tool)#Cultural associations|rake]]; a smaller, handheld, decorated version is sold as an ''engimono'' and is believed to be able to, literally, rake-in good-fortune &/or rake-out bad-fortune for the user. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|cloud}}||{{ill|Cloud|en|Cloud|qid=Q8074|lt=Kumo|1=Cloud}}}}''' – Synonymous with heaven; in the event that a household ''kamidana'' cannot be installed in the highest point of the house, the ''Kanji'' for 'Cloud' is written on a piece of paper and affixed above the ''kamidana''; doing this lets the ''kami'' know that, while they should be enshrined at the highest point, circumstances prevent this from being-so. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Kunitokotachi|Kunitokotachi-no-mikoto]]}}''' – A ''kami'' considered to be the most important by [[Yoshida Kanetomo]] and considered important also by Watarai Shinto.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo||/|Kunitsu tsumi}}''' – A term for ''tsumi'' specifically committed on Earth. The corresponding concept is ''amatsu tsumi''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Kushi-mitama|1=Mitama}}}}''' – The wise and experienced side of a ''mitama''. The ''kushi-mitama'' is associated with the colours blue and green, and with the cardinal direction of east. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|medicine ball}}||{{ill|Kusudama|en|Kusudama|qid=Q1191157|lt=Kusudama|1=Kusudama}}}}''' – Originating from ancient Japanese culture, ''kusudama'' were used for {{ill|Incense|en|Incense|qid=Q273131|lt=Incense|1=Incense}} and {{ill|Potpourri|en|Potpourri|qid=Q920748|lt=Potpourri|1=Potpourri}}; possibly originally being actual bunches of flowers or herbs. They are now typically used as decorations or as gifts. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[:en:Mulberry|Mulberry]] field}}||{{ill|Kuwabara kuwabara|en|Kuwabara kuwabara|qid=Q3817558|lt=Kuwabara kuwabara|1=Kuwabara kuwabara}}}}''' – A phrase used in the Japanese language to ward off lightning. It is analogous to the English phrase "knock on wood" to prevent bad luck. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|sect Shinto}}||Kyōha Shinto}}''' – A label applied to certain sects by the Meiji government to give them an official status.<ref name="bocking" /> * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hell of Screaming}}||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Kyōkan Jigoku]]}}''' – The fourth level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners, who have committed murder, theft, degeneration, and drunkenness are sent.
==Gallery: I to K== <gallery> Image:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|A man confronted with an apparition of Inari, appearing in this case as a woman Image:Meotoiwa wedded rocks.jpg|An ''iwakura'' (the [[Meoto Iwa]]) girdled by a ''shimenawa'' Image:AsakusaShrine1439.jpg|A ''jinja'' (shrine), characterized by the presence of a ''torii'' Image:Kannushi and miko at the Meiji Shrine, Tokyo.jpg|A ''kannushi'' (right) wearing a ''jōe'' Image:TsurugaokaHachiman4226.jpg|A ''kaguraden'' Image:Kamidana.jpg|A ''kamidana'' Image:Kannushi Kamakura.jpg|A ''kannushi'' during a wedding Image:Naiku 01.JPG|''Katsuogi'' (poles perpendicular to the roof ridge) at Ise Shrine Image:Fox0290.jpg|Statue of a fox, messenger of ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} Image:ItsukushimaKomainu7374.jpg|A ''komainu'' </gallery>
==M== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|genuine seabream}}||{{ill|Pagrus major|en|Pagrus major|qid=Q1196387|lt=Madai|1=Pagrus major}}}}''' – A fish native to Japan. It is also featured among ''Engimono'' (above), as its red colour is seen as [[Color in Chinese culture#Red / Vermilion|auspicious]]. The ''madai'' is often seen with {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, as he is the patron ''kami'' of fisherman and one of the Seven Lucky Gods. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|curved jewel}}| or |[[Magatama]]*}}''' – A comma-shaped jewel, often used as a ''yorishiro''. See also ''Yasakani no Magatama''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|honesty}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Makoto]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|qid=Q7800836|lt=Manju|1=Tide jewels}}}}''' – One of two magical gems that Ryūjin used to control the tides; its counterpart is the ''Kanju''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|beckoning cat}}||[[Maneki-neko]]}}''' – A protective and good luck talisman. * '''{{nihongo3|| or |{{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|qid=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}}}''' – A concept of a spiritual or divine being that visits from the 'other world' (takai, a term for the Japanese afterlife) at specific times and, thus, must be shown the height of {{ill|Hospitality|en|Hospitality|qid=Q815825|lt=Hospitality|1=Hospitality}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|descendant shrine}}||[[Setsumatsusha|Massha]]*}}''' – A synonym of ''sessha''. * '''{{nihongo|||Mayoke}}''' – A ward against evil. * '''{{nihongo|||Meido}}''' – Another name for Yomi. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|dark world}}||Meikai}}''' – Another word for Yomi. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Honour|en|Honour|qid=Q207333|lt=Honour|1=Honour}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Meiyo]]}}''' – One of the seven-plus [[:en:Virtues|Virtues]] of ''{{ill|Bushido|en|Bushido|qid=Q183738|lt=Bushido|1=Bushido}}''. * '''{{nihongo||, , or |[[Miko]]*}}''' – A woman who helps ''kannushi'' in their work, or a woman possessing magic powers and capable of giving fortunes. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Mikoshi]]}}''' – A divine palanquin. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Misogi]]*}}''' – An ascetic practice of ritual ablution purification. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|honourable soul}}|, or |{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}}}''' – The spirit of a ''kami'' or the soul of a dead person.<ref name=kojien>Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version</ref> The opposite of ''mitama'' is ''tamashii''. See also ''ara-mitama'', ''kushi-mitama'', ''nigi-mitama'' and ''saki-mitama''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|palace}}||Miya}}''' – A term that often defines a shrine enshrining a special ''kami'' or a member of the Imperial household, for example an empress. See also ''Ō-miya'' (below) * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|shrine visit}}||[[Miyamairi]]}}''' – A traditional rite of passage for newborns held at a shrine. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|water orb}}||Mizutama}}''' – A small, droplet-shaped vessel, used for holding offerings of fresh water on a ''kamidama'', and is to be changed daily; belongs to the ''shingu'' for holding offerings. * '''{{nihongo||, もちつき|[[Mochi#Seasonal specialties|Mochitsuki]]}}''' – A traditional activity for occasions like Japanese New-Year, rice is pounded into ''{{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|qid=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}}'' and is eaten in hopes of gaining good fortune over the coming year. The activity is associated with the moon, jade, or golden rabbit, which according to East-Asian folklore, is said to pound rice (or the {{ill|Elixir of life|en|Elixir of life|qid=Q912502|lt=Elixir of life|1=Elixir of life}}) in its [[mortar and pestle]], at the behest of the ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|red leaf hunting}}||[[Leaf peeping#Japan|Momijigari]]}}''' – The Japanese traditional festival and custom of enjoying the transient beauty of leaves changing colour in the autumn; the Japanese tradition of going to visit scenic areas where leaves have turned red in the autumn; particularly {{ill|Maple|en|Maple|qid=Q42292|lt=Maple|1=Maple}} tree leaves. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Mononoke|en|Mononoke|qid=Q11570636|lt=Mononoke|1=Mononoke}}}}''' – A {{ill|Monster|en|Monster|qid=Q276453|lt=Monster|1=Monster}}ous apparition; a monster. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|forest}}| or |Mori}}''' – A wood, a forest, a grove, specifically a grove or forest on shrine grounds. It reflects close relationship between trees and shrines. Tree worship is common in Shinto. * '''{{nihongo|||Muenbotoke}}''' – The soul or spirit of a deceased human with no living connections amongst the living; the dead who have no living relatives); similarly to ''gaki'' and ''jikininki'', a ''muenbotoke'' can be appeased by a ''sagaki''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Avīci|en|Avīci|qid=Q2629117|lt=Mugen Jigoku|1=Avīci}}}}''' – The eighth and deepest level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners that have committed murder, theft, degeneration, drunkenness, lying, blasphemy, and rape, {{ill|Parricide|en|Parricide|qid=Q51159758|lt=Parricide|1=Parricide}}, and assassination of holy men are sent. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|deity of binding}}||Musubi-no-Kami}}''' – One of the Shinto ''kami'' of creation; also known as the ''kami'' of matchmaking, love, and marriages. * '''{{nihongo|||Musubi}}''' – A term in Shinto for the spiritual influences that produces all the things in the universe and helps them develop and complete their cycle. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|bright deity}}||Myōjin}}''' – A term that refers to a title, historically applied to ''kami'' and their shrines. See also ''sannō''.
==N== * '''{{nihongo|||[[Hinamatsuri#Customs|Nagashi-bina]]}}''' – Ritual purification ceremonies held where participants make dolls out of materials such as paper or straw and send them on a boat down a river, carrying one's impurities and sin with them. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|weeding blade}}||{{ill|Naginata|en|Naginata|qid=Q546116|lt=Naginata|1=Naginata}}}}''' – A {{ill|Polearm|en|Polearm|qid=Q649107|lt=Polearm|1=Polearm}}, sometimes dedicated to shrines (along with other weapons, such as ''katana'' and spears), and indicative of a ''kami's'' power to ward off negative influences. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|raw peel}}||{{ill|Namahage|en|Namahage|qid=Q1133889|lt=Namahage|1=Namahage}}}}''' – In traditional [[Japanese folklore]] is a demon-like being, portrayed by men wearing hefty ''oni'' masks and traditional straw capes during a New Year's ritual. * '''{{nihongo|||Nanakusa-gayū}}''' – A traditional meal of congee and herbs served on ''Nanakusa-no-Sekku''. The seven herbs are {{ill|Oenanthe javanica|en|Oenanthe javanica|qid=Q905974|lt=Japanese parsley|1=Oenanthe javanica}}, [[:en:Shepherd's purse|Shepherd's purse]], ''{{ill|Pseudognaphalium affine|en|Pseudognaphalium affine|qid=Q15598708|lt=Pseudognaphalium affine|1=Pseudognaphalium affine}}'', [[:en:Chickweed|Chickweed]], [[:en:Nipplewort|Nipplewort]], turnips, and {{ill|Daikon|en|Daikon|qid=Q7224757|lt=Daikon|1=Daikon}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|seven-herb festival}}||{{ill|Nanakusa-no-sekku|en|Nanakusa-no-sekku|qid=Q612101|lt=Nanakusa-no-Sekku|1=Nanakusa-no-sekku}}}}''' – One of the five annual traditional festivals held throughout the year. It is observed on 7 January. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|seven spiritual mountains}}||Nanareizan}}''' – Seven mountains revered as sacred in Shinto and Buddhism; they are: Mount Fuji, Mount Haku, Mount Tate, {{ill|Mount Ōmine|en|Mount Ōmine|qid=Q3695989|lt=Mount Ōmine|1=Mount Ōmine}}, [[Mount Shakka]], {{ill|Mount Daisen|en|Mount Daisen|qid=Q715498|lt=Mount Daisen|1=Mount Daisen}}, and {{ill|Mount Ishizuchi|en|Mount Ishizuchi|qid=Q2744214|lt=Mount Ishizuchi|1=Mount Ishizuchi}}. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Naraka (Buddhism)|en|Naraka (Buddhism)|qid=Q1148173|lt=Naraku|1=Naraka (Buddhism)}}}}''' – The Hell realm of incarnation, the lowest and worst realm on the wheel of reincarnation.
