See ''rinne''. * '''{{nihongo|| {{lit|thought beads}}|{{ill|Japamala|en|Japamala|qid=Q1336519|lt=Nenju|1=Japamala}}}}''' – Shinto-Buddhist {{ill|Rosary|en|Rosary|qid=Q132539|lt=Rosary|1=Rosary}}; a string or necklace of beads used for prayers. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|land of roots}}||{{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|qid=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}}}''' – A term referring to a netherworld or limbo in Japanese mythology, like the Sanzu River. It is sometimes considered to be identical to Yomi, but darker, as well as ''Tokoyo-no-kuni''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|tranquil soul}}||{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Nigi-mitama|1=Mitama}}}}''' – The friendly and cooperative side of a complete ''mitama''. It is associated with the colour white and the cardinal direction of west. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Nihon Shoki]]}}''' – An early Japanese written chronicle of myths, legends, songs, genealogies, oral traditions, and semi-historical accounts down to 720 AD. Similar to the ''Kojiki''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Human beings in Buddhism|en|Human beings in Buddhism|qid=Q5937705|lt=Ningen|1=Human beings in Buddhism}}}}''' – The human realm of incarnation, the third-highest realm on the wheel of reincarnation. See ''Rinne'' (below). * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|qid=Q539715|lt=Nio|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}}}''' – In Shinto-Buddhism, ''nio'' is the Japanese name for the ''Kongōrikishi'', the two wrathful and muscular guardians of the Buddha standing at the entrance of many Buddhist temples. See also ''a-un'', and Gozu and Mezu. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|invocation scripts}}||[[Norito]]}}''' – [[liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in Shinto, usually addressed to a given ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|blessing}}||Norito}}''' – Written spells for blessings. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|curse}}|, & |Noroi}}''' – Written spells for curses. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Noshi|en|Noshi|qid=Q1511688|lt=Noshi|1=Noshi}}}}''' – Ceremonial pieces of paper wrapped in a sheet of coloured paper folded in a long hexagonal shape that are attached to gifts and presents offered on festive occasions in Japan. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Nusa|1=Ōnusa}}}}''' – See ''ōnusa''; a wooden wand used in Shinto rituals. It is decorated with many ''shide''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|As-One-Wishes Jewel}}||[[Cintamani|Nyoihōju]]}}''' – A wish-fulfilling jewel within both {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|qid=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} traditions, and the Eastern equivalent of the {{ill|Philosopher's stone|en|Philosopher's stone|qid=Q182053|lt=Philosopher's stone|1=Philosopher's stone}} in Western alchemy. It is one of several {{ill|Mani Jewel|en|Mani Jewel|qid=Q24906547|lt=Mani Jewel|1=Mani Jewel}} images found in Buddhist scripture. The ''Nyoihōju'' is commonly-depicted within the hands of Kisshōten, as-well-as in the hands of a bodhisattva in art.
==O== * '''{{nihongo|| or |Obake}}''' – An apparition or spectre. * '''{{nihongo|| or |[[Ofuda]]}}''' – Written paper spells, amulets and talismans. See also ''omamori''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hyottoko|en|Hyottoko|qid=Q795293|lt=Okame|1=Hyottoko}}}}''' – Also called Otafuku. It is a mask that depicts the face of a woman who has a short nose and swollen round cheeks. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Rice#Cultural roles of rice|O-kome]]}}''' – Offerings of white rice made at Shinto shrines and a household's ''kamidana''. See also ''kome''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|first food}}||[[Childbirth in Japan#Newborn|Ō-kuizome]]}}''' – When a baby is 100 days old, Japanese families celebrate a weaning ceremony called ''Ō-Kuizome''. This ceremony traditionally involves a large shared meal prepared by the mother-in-law. * '''[[Ōkuninushi]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Master of the Great Land|Great Master of the Land}}) – see Daikokuten. * '''{{nihongo|| and |Ōmagatoki}}''' – A term referring to the moment at twilight, when the sky grows dark. ''Ōmagatoki'' is the time when ''chimimōryō'', the evil spirits of the mountains and rivers, attempt to materialise in the World of the Living. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Omamori|en|Omamori|qid=Q1756788|lt=Omamori|1=Omamori}}}}''' – {{ill|Amulet|en|Amulet|qid=Q131557|lt=Amulet|1=Amulet}}s and {{ill|Talisman|en|Talisman|qid=Q44506|lt=Talisman|1=Talisman}}s available at shrines and temples for particular purposes, for example health or success in business. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|small mourning robes}}||Omigoro}}''' – An overcoat robe used for Shinto services. * '''{{nihongo|| or |[[Sake#Ceremonial use|Ō-miki]]}}''' – Offerings of white rice wine made at Shinto shrines and a household's ''kamidana''. It is often consumed as part of Shinto purification rituals. See also ''sake''. * '''{{nihongo|| or |{{ill|O-mikuji|en|O-mikuji|qid=Q1069592|lt=O-mikuji|1=O-mikuji}}*}}''' – fortunes written on strips of paper that are often found at shrines wrapped around tree branches. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|qid=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}}}''' – In one interpretation of an ''oni'', they are a kind of ''yōkai'', whereas another interpretation of an ''oni'' is as something completely separate from a ''yōkai'' (although both are supernatural monsters). See also ''jikininki'' and ''gaki''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Kijo (folklore)|en|Kijo (folklore)|qid=Q6142096|lt=Onibabā|1=Kijo (folklore)}}}}''' – An ''oni'' woman from Japanese legends. See also ''kijo'' and ''yama-uba''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Onibi|en|Onibi|qid=Q11673509|lt=Onibi|1=Onibi}}}}''' – A type of atmospheric ghost light in legends of Japan; according to folklore, they can be anything from fires caused by ''oni'', to the spirits born from the corpses of humans and animals. They are also said to be resentful people that have become fire and appeared. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Oni Tile(s)}}||{{ill|Onigawara|en|Onigawara|qid=Q1895128|lt=Onigawara|1=Onigawara}}}}''' – A type of roof ornamentation found in Japanese architecture. They are generally roof tiles or statues depicting an ''oni''. Prior to the Heian period, similar ornaments with floral and plant designs (''hanagawara'') preceded the ''onigawara''. * '''{{nihongo|||Oni hitokuchi}}''' – A term similar to ''kamikakushi'' and ''tengukakushi'' (below), referring-to the mysterious disappearance or death of a person without warning or without a trace. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Demons Out! Luck In!}}||[[Setsubun|Oni-wa-Soto! Fuku-wa-Uchi!]]}}''' – A mantra for driving-off malevolent spirits, demons or devils that cause ill fortune to allow good fortune to work unimpeded. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|yin-yang way}}||[[Onmyōdō]]}}''' – A traditional Japanese [[Japanese creation myth|esoteric cosmology]]; a mixture of natural science and occultism. * '''[[Onmyōji]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|yin-yang practitioner}}) – A practitioner of ''onmyōdō''. * '''{{nihongo|||Onmyōryō}}''' – A governmental office of ''onmyōdō'' that was responsible for timekeeping and calendar-making. They also documented and analysed omens and fortunes. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|vengeful spirit}}||[[Onryō]]}}''' – A type of {{ill|Vengeful ghost|en|Vengeful ghost|qid=Q2836709|lt=vengeful spirit|1=Vengeful ghost}}; a {{ill|Poltergeist|en|Poltergeist|qid=Q216893|lt=Poltergeist|1=Poltergeist}}. * '''{{nihongo|| or |{{ill|Japamala|en|Japamala|qid=Q1336519|lt=Ō-Nenju|1=Japamala}}}}''' – A string of prayer beads commonly used in Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, Buddhism and Shinto for the spiritual practice known in Sanskrit as ''{{ill|Japa|en|Japa|qid=Q1683111|lt=Japa|1=Japa}}''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Ōnusa|en|Ōnusa|qid=Q1153887|lt=Ō-Nusa|1=Ōnusa}}}}''' – Wooden wands used in rituals. Decorated with many ''shide'', they are waved left and right during ceremonies. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|season}}||{{ill|Osechi|en|Osechi|qid=Q862557|lt=Osechi|1=Osechi}}}}''' – Also known as ''osechi-ryōri'' (御節料理 or お節料理), an ''osechi'' is a traditional spread of Japanese New Year foods. The tradition started in the Heian period (794–1185). ''Osechi'' are recognizable by their ''{{ill|Jūbako|en|Jūbako|qid=Q11644839|lt=Jūbako|1=Jūbako}}'' (重箱), which resemble ''bentō'' boxes. ''Osechi'' includes dishes like ''ozōni''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|seventh night}}||[[Childbirth in Japan#Newborn|O-Shichiya]]}}''' – When a baby turns seven days old, families officially name the baby. The mother, the father, and the grandparents are often involved in this process. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Hyottoko|en|Hyottoko|qid=Q795293|lt=Otafuku|1=Hyottoko}}}}''' – A mask that represents a smiling Japanese woman with large, rosy cheeks. ''Otafuku'' is also known as Ame-no-Uzume * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Ō-yashiro|1=Shinto shrine}}}}''' – See also ''Taisha''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Great Tray}}||{{ill|Plinth|en|Plinth|qid=Q268205|lt=Ozen|1=Plinth}}}}''' – A traditional Japanese four-legged tray used to carry food offerings.
==Gallery: M to O== <gallery> Image:Magatama.png|Jōmon period ''magatama'' Image:Katsuragi-jinja (Gose, Nara) massha.jpg|''Massha'' at Katsuragi Shrine in Gose, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} Image:Miko at Kasuga Taisha.jpg|A ''miko'' at [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] Image:Yaho Temmangu Festival 2002 d.JPG|A ''mikoshi'' Image:Jishu-Omamori.jpg|''Omamori'' on sale Image:NikkoPaperOffering5174.jpg|An ''ōnusa'' Image:KasugaTaisha2.jpg|Tying ''omikuji'' at [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] in Nara </gallery>
==R== * '''{{nihongo|[[Raijin]]|}}''' – The ''kami'' of {{ill|Thunder|en|Thunder|qid=Q83301|lt=Thunder|1=Thunder}} and {{ill|Lightning|en|Lightning|qid=Q33741|lt=Lightning|1=Lightning}} who is the brother of Fūjin; together, they are both said to be two of the many sons and other children of Izanagi and Izanami. * '''{{nihongo|||Rakan}}''' – An ''{{ill|Arhat|en|Arhat|qid=Q225932|lt=Arhat|1=Arhat}}'', a Buddhist saint. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|etiquette}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Rei]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spirit}}||Rei}}''' – The soul or spirit. * '''{{nihongo|||Reibai}}''' – A person who can sense the presence of spirits and other supernatural/paranormal phenomena. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spirit world}}||Reikai}}''' – See ''seishinkai''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spirit sense}}||Reikan}}''' – The ability to sense the presence of spirits and other supernatural/paranormal phenomena. * '''{{nihongo|||Reiki}}''' – The soul or spirit of someone or something dead, particularly malevolent. * '''{{nihongo|||Reikon}}''' – The soul or spirit of a person, usually someone who has just died. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Spirit Sensing Prowess}}||Reinōryoku}}''' – The ability to sense the presence of spirits and other supernatural/paranormal phenomena; the term for psychics in Japan is {{nihongo3|{{lit|Spiritual Ability Person}}||Reinōsha}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Spirit Mountain}}||Reizan}}''' – A holy mountain. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Standing bell|en|Standing bell|qid=Q1413983|lt=Rin|1=Standing bell}}}}''' – A type of standing bell or resting bell. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Saṃsāra (Buddhism)|en|Saṃsāra (Buddhism)|qid=Q10993736|lt=Rinne|1=Saṃsāra (Buddhism)}}}}''' – The concept of rebirth; the beginning-less cycle of repeated birth from within six realms of reincarnation, mundane existence and dying again. * '''''[[Japanese calendar#Rokuyō|Rokuyō]]''''' (), the six-day week of the ''Shinto-{{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}}'' {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=Calendar|1=Calendar}}. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Ryōbu Shintō|Ryōbu Shintō]]}}''' – Also called ''shingon Shintō'', in Japanese religion, the syncretic school that combined Shinto with the teachings of the ''Shingon'' sect of ''Buddhism''. The school developed during the late Heian and Kamakura periods. The basis of the school's beliefs was the Japanese concept that ''kami'' were manifestations of Buddhist divinities. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Dragon God}}||[[Ryūjin]]}}''' – The {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} of the seas and oceans in [[Shinto]] {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|qid=Q12827256|lt=Myth|1=Myth}}.
==S== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|festival podium}}||Saidan}}''' – An altar or a structure to make offerings to ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||Saikigū}}''' – The utensils used in religious ceremonies, including the ''sanbō'', ''oshiki'', ''hassoku-an'', and ''takatsuki''. * '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=Cleyera japonica|1=Cleyera japonica}}*|}}''' – A type of flowering evergreen tree native to Japan that is sacred in Shinto. Cuttings of sakaki, called ''tamagushi'', are often offered as offerings at shrines and in rituals. Cuttings are also displayed on either side of a ''kamidana'' as offerings. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Sakaki Stands}}||Sakaki-tate}}''' – A pair of small, white vases, used for displaying cuttings of sakaki are displayed on either side of a ''kamidana'' as offerings and at rituals. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Sake]]}}''' – An alcoholic beverage made by fermenting white rice. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=Saki-mitama|1=Mitama}}}}''' – The happy and loving side of a complete ''mitama''. It is associated with the colours red and pink, and with the cardinal direction of south. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|three directions}}||Sanbō}}''' – A stand used to bear food offerings, usually made of unpainted [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]]. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Saisen|en|Saisen|qid=Q988479|lt=Saisen|1=Saisen}}}}''' – Offerings of money given by worshipers. * {{Nihongo|'''Saishi'''|}} - rituals.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Shintoism Facts |url=https://www.softschools.com/facts/religion/shintoism_facts/2738/ |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=www.softschools.com}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=17 Facts You Probably Didn't Know About Shintoism |url=https://www.tsunagujapan.com/17-facts-you-probably-didnt-know-about-shintoism/ |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=tsunagu Japan |date=8 February 2022 |language=en}}</ref> * '''{{nihongo|||Sanbon Jime}}''' – A custom in Japan; rhythmic-clapping hands—and shouting-loudly—when celebrating. This is done when people are celebrating the successful end of an event. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}*}}''' – The path leading from a ''torii'' to a shrine. The term is also used sometimes at Buddhist temples too. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Mountain King}}||Sannō}}''' – A term that refers to, both a title for a type of ''kami'', and a specific divine spirit that protects a divine mountain. * '''{{nihongo|||Sanpai sahō}}''' – The way in which the Japanese worship at shrines, bowing twice, clapping twice, then bowing one last time. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|three spiritual mountains}}||{{ill|Three Holy Mountains|en|Three Holy Mountains|qid=Q11086198|lt=Sanreizan|1=Three Holy Mountains}}}}''' – Three mountains revered as sacred in Shinto: Mount Fuji, Mount Haku, and Mount Tate. They are included amongst the ''Nanareizan''. * '''[[Sanzu River]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|River of Three Crossings}}) – A mythological river that acts as a boundary between the lands of the living and the dead. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|vitality}}||Sei}}''' – The energy of living things. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[hallow]]ed ground}}||Seichi}}''' – Another word for ''jōdo''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Coming of Age Day|en|Coming of Age Day|qid=Q483226|lt=Seijin-no-Hi|1=Coming of Age Day}}}}''' – A Japanese holiday held annually on the second Monday of January. It is held to congratulate and encourage all those who have reached or will reach the age of maturity between 2 April of the previous year and 1 April of the current year. * '''{{nihongo||/|[[Platycodon#Japan|Seimei Kikyō]]}}''' – A pentacle that is the personal seal of [[Abe no Seimei|Abe-no-Seimei]], later becoming the symbol for the ''Onmyōryō'' (the government ministry department for the practice of ''onmyōdō'', or the Bureau of Taoist Geomancy) and for ''onmyōdō'' itself, given its association with the Five Elements. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Soul}}||Seirei}}''' – A general umbrella term for the essence, spirit or soul of a nonspecific thing. * '''{{nihongo|||Seisatsu*}}''' – A signboard containing announcements and rules for worshipers. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spirit world}}||{{ill|Spirit world (Spiritualism)|en|Spirit world (Spiritualism)|qid=Q7578061|lt=Seishinkai|1=Spirit world (Spiritualism)}}}}''' – A spiritual, non-corporeal world that coexists with the material world, that human beings inhabit, but in a different dimension. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|thosuand shrine tags}}||[[Senjafuda]]}}''' – [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, stickers or placards (and ''ofuda'') posted on the gates or buildings of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Setsumatsusha|Sessha]]*}}''' – A smaller shrine housing a ''kami'' having a strong relationship with that of the ''honsha'' (the main shrine). A synonym of ''massha''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|seasonal divide}}||[[Setsubun]]}}''' – A ceremony held on February 3 that celebrates the beginning of spring in Japan. * '''{{nihongo|||[[:en:Shaku (ritual baton)|Shaku]]}}''' – A flat baton often seen in portraits of noblemen and samurai, but also used by ''kannushi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Shrine Business Establishment}}||Shamusho}}''' – A shrine's administrative office. It often sells ''omamori'' and other goods. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|seven-five-three}}||[[Shichi-Go-San]]}}''' – A traditional {{ill|Rite of passage|en|Rite of passage|qid=Q1131696|lt=Rite of passage|1=Rite of passage}} and festival day in Japan for three- and seven-year-old girls and five-year-old boys, held annually on November 15. * '''{{nihongo||, and |{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|qid=Q2657982|lt=Shide|1=Shide (Shinto)}}*}}''' – A zigzag-shaped paper streamer often attached to a ''shimenawa'' and to ''tamagushi'', and is used in rituals. * '''{{nihongo|||Shigai}}''' – [[Moccasin]]-like footwear, made from silk with leather soles. Originally worn by children and young woman of the aristocratic-class, it is now (or, at-least was) worn by ''miko'' in Shinto rituals. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Deer|en|Deer|qid=Q23390|lt=Shika|1=Deer}}}}''' – A deer. In Shinto, they are considered messengers of the ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shikome|en|Shikome|qid=Q2390449|lt=Shikome|1=Shikome}}}}''' – Hags sent by Izanami to pursue Izanagi for shaming her. Their numbers differ between the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. * '''{{nihongo|||Shinbai}}''' – A divine spiritualist or medium. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine punishment}}||Shinbatsu}}''' – Punishment dealt-out by a ''kami'' against transgressions committed by mortals and immortals alike. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine tree}}||Shinboku}}''' – A tree considered divine. * '''{{nihongo|||Shimekazari}}''' – A {{ill|Wreath|en|Wreath|qid=Q1201922|lt=Wreath|1=Wreath}} made of braided-''shimenawa'' rope with auspicious additions interwoven into it. ''Shimekazari'' are put up as decorations for [[Japanese New Year]], along with ''{{ill|Kadomatsu|en|Kadomatsu|qid=Q2667233|lt=Kadomatsu|1=Kadomatsu}}''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Enclosing Rope}}| / / |{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}}}''' – Lengths of rope made from laid/woven/braided rice straw or hemp that are used for ritual purification. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine gardens}}||Shinen}}''' – The gardens on shrine grounds. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine tool}}||Shingu}}''' – A term for the items displayed upon a ''kamidana'' (see above). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|dead soul}}||Shinidama}}''' – The soul of a dead person. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine domain}}||Shiniki}}''' – The perimeters of a shrine or a place where ''kami'' dwell. It is also used to refer to any place of significant importance. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine service}}||Shinji}}''' – Rituals and services in Shinto. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine official}}||{{ill|Shinkan (official)|en|Shinkan (official)|qid=Q11589454|lt=Shinkan|1=Shinkan (official)}}}}''' – A person serving a certain god or working as a government official at a facility where God is worshipped. ''Shinkan'' is also used as a term designating a Shinto priest (a person involved in religious services and office work in a shrine). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine fox}}||Shinko}}''' – Similar to ''komainu'', ''shinko'' are twin dual statues of foxes, usually depicted as having white fur, who serve Inari Ōkami as messengers. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine mirror}}||Shinkyō}}''' – A mirror that is often included in shrine altars and home altars; they are believed to represent the ''kami'' themselves. * '''{{nihongo|||Shinra Banshō}}''' – A Shinto-Buddhist term, meaning "all things existing in the universe": According to Shinto, the ''kami'' exist within ''shinra banshō''. * '''{{nihongo|||Shinsōsai}}''' – A Shinto funeral service. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine seat}}||Shinza}}''' – A place where there is a ''kami'' or a place where housing the sacred object of a shrine, like a ''{{ill|Sanctum sanctorum|en|Sanctum sanctorum|qid=Q7415734|lt=Sanctum sanctorum|1=Sanctum sanctorum}}''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shinto wedding|en|Shinto wedding|qid=Q18215243|lt=Shinzen-Kekkonshiki|1=Shinto wedding}}}}''' – A Shinto wedding ceremony. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|salt}}||[[Salt#In religion|Shio]]}}''' – In Shinto, salt is used for ritual purification of locations and people (harae, specifically ''[[Harae#Practice|shubatsu]]'' ()), and small piles of salt are placed in dishes by the entrance of establishments for the two-fold purposes of warding off evil and attracting patrons. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Shinbutsu bunri]]}}''' – The legal forbiddance of the {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} between Shinto and Buddhism. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|dead spirit}}||[[Shiryō|Shiryō/shirei]]}}''' – The spirit of a dead person. The terms are used in contrast to ''ikiryō'', which refers to a disembodied spirit that leaves the body of a still-living person and haunts other people or places, sometimes across great distances. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|divine body}}||[[Shintai]]}}''' – A sacred object, but also natural objects such as trees and mountains, which represents the ''kami'' for worship. * '''Shrine''' – The English word that translates several more specialized Japanese words (see article [[Shinto shrine#Shōgō|Shinto shrine]]). Any structure housing a ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shinbutsu kakuri|en|Shinbutsu kakuri|qid=Q3482168|lt=Shinbutsu kakuri|1=Shinbutsu kakuri}}}}''' – The tendency in medieval and early modern Japan to keep particular ''kami'' separate from any form or manifestation of Buddhism. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]}}''' – The syncretism of Buddhism and local religious beliefs, the normal state of things before the ''shinbutsu bunri''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Shinsen]]}}''' – Offerings of foods given to Shinto shrines or ''kamidana'' for the ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Salt#In religion|Shio]]}}''' – In Shinto, {{ill|Salt|en|Salt|qid=Q11254|lt=Salt|1=Salt}} is used for offerings at Shinto shrines and a household's ''kamidana'', [[Ritual purification#Shinto|ritual purification]] of locations and people, and small piles of salt are placed in dishes by the entrance of establishments for the twofold purpose of warding off evil and attracting patrons. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hallowed ground}}||Shōdo}}''' – See also ''jōdo'' (see above) and ''seichi'' (see above). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hell of Burning}}||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Shonetsu Jigoku]]}}''' – The third level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners who have committed murder, theft, and degeneration are sent. * '''{{nihongo|||Shubatsu}}''' – A ceremony involving salt that is conducted immediately prior to rituals in order to purify participants, food offerings and ''tamagushi'', of sins and defilement. See also ''misogi''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Shugenja|en|Shugenja|qid=Q7504680|lt=Shugenja|1=Shugenja}}}}''' – A practitioner of ''shugendō''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Naraka (Buddhism)#Hot Narakas|Shugo Jigoku]]}}''' – The sixth level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners who have committed murder, theft, degeneration, drunkenness, lying, and blasphemy are sent. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[[:en:Vermillion|Vermillion]] seal}}||[[Shuin]]}}''' – A commemorative seal stamp given to worshippers and visitors to Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples in Japan. The seal stamps are often collected in ''shuinchō'' that are sold at shrines and temples. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|vermillion seal book}}||Shuinchō}}''' – A book used to collect ''shuin''. * '''{{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}''' – A term first used after World War II to broadly classify Shinto ideals, rituals and institutions created by the pre-war government to promote the divinity of the emperor and the uniqueness of Japan. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Asura (Buddhism)|Shura]]}}''' – The semi-divine/Ashura realm of incarnation, the second-highest realm on the wheel of reincarnation. See also ''rinne''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|[Buddhist] Monk Warrior}}||[[Sōhei]]}}''' – A ''Sōhei'' is a practitioner of ''Shugendō''. ''Sōhei'' are also known as a ''{{ill|Shugenja|en|Shugenja|qid=Q7504680|lt=Shugenja|1=Shugenja}}'' (above), and a ''[[Yamabushi]]'' (below); a kind of mountain {{ill|Hermit|en|Hermit|qid=Q189829|lt=Hermit|1=Hermit}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|ancestral spirits}}||[[Sorei]]}}''' – A term in Shinto-Buddhism that refers to the [[Veneration of the dead#Japan|spirits of one's ancestors]]. * '''{{nihongo|||Suikan}}''' – An informal garment, like a tunic, worn by males of the Japanese nobility in the Heian period, as outerwear; an informal garment, like a tunic, worn by males of the Japanese nobility in the Heian period, as outerwear. * '''{{nihongo|| / |[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]}}''' – The multifaceted ''kami'' of storms, the sea, open fields, the harvest, marriage, and love, the son of Izanagi and the younger brother of Amaterasu Ōkami and Tsukuyomi. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|bell}}||[[Suzu (bell)|Suzu]]}}''' – A round, hollow Shinto bell that contains pellets that sound when agitated.
==Gallery: R to S== <gallery> Image:Saisen.jpg|A ''{{ill|Saisen|en|Saisen|qid=Q988479|lt=saisen-bako|1=Saisen}}'', or offertory box Image:Sakaki branch.jpg|''Sakaki'' branches Image:Osaka-gokoku-jinja1.jpg|A ''sandō'' (a shrine's approach) Image:Seisatsu.jpg|A ''seisatsu'' Image:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Geku) 03.JPG|''Sessha'' (Tsukkomi no Miya) at [[Ise Grand Shrine]] File:Kamogawa ceremony 01.jpg|Two ''kannushi'' holding a ''shaku'' File:Shinto Hemp.jpg|Two ''shide'' decorating a small shrine Image:Izumooyashiro89.JPG|A ''shimenawa'' Image:Yuki_Shrine_-_giant_Sugi.jpg|A ''shimboku'' girdled by a ''shimenawa'' </gallery>
==T== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|great drum}}||{{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|qid=Q221769|lt=Taiko|1=Taiko}}}}''' – A term for a broad range of Japanese percussion drums. The ''taiko'' drum has been an integral part of Japanese [[Shinto]] and {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} religious practices and [[Japanese folklore|folklore]] for centuries. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|large shrine}}||{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Taisha|1=Shinto shrine}}}}''' – A term usually used as a part of the official name of a shrine, as for example in [[Izumo-taisha]]. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|large shrine architecture}}||[[Taisha-zukuri]]}}''' – The oldest style of shrine architecture used for example at Izumo-taisha. * '''[[Takamagahara]]''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Plains of the High Heaven}}) – In Shinto, the dwelling place of the heavenly gods, the ''Amatsukami''. It is believed to be connected to the Earth by ''Ama-no-uki-hashi''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|treasure ship}}||{{ill|Takarabune|en|Takarabune|qid=Q48723823|lt=Takarabune|1=Takarabune}}}}''' – A mythical ship piloted through the heavens by the Seven Lucky Gods during the first three days of the New Year. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|jewel fence}}||{{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|qid=Q3078312|lt=Tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}}}}''' – The fence delimiting the sacred soil of a shrine. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|jewel skewer}}||[[Tamagushi]]*}}''' – A form of offering made from a ''sakaki''-tree branch and strips of paper, silk, or cotton. * '''{{nihongo|||Tamashii}}''' – A soul within its proper body, encompassing one's mind, heart and soul. The opposite of ''tamashii'' is ''mitama''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spirit house}}||[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}}''' – An altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship, dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Japanese raccoon dog|en|Japanese raccoon dog|qid=Q122835073|lt=Tanuki|1=Japanese raccoon dog}}}}''' – A mammal native to Japan (and Asia (as a subspecies of the [[:en:Raccoon dog|Asian raccoon dog]])) and to Japanese culture and folklore since ancient times. It is known in folklore to be mischievous and jolly, a master of disguise, and with a taste for ''sake''. It is also regarded as a Japanese art animal, appearing in many different forms of both modern and traditional art. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Tanabata#Customs|Tanzaku]]}}''' – A small strip of paper that wishes are written on, and is hung on bamboo or other trees during ''{{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|qid=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}}''. * '''{{nihongo|||Tatarigami}}''' – Powerful evil spirits that bring calamity. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|shield}}||Tate}}''' – A {{ill|Shield|en|Shield|qid=Q131559|lt=Shield|1=Shield}}, sometimes dedicated to shrines, and indicative of a ''kami's'' power to ward-off and protect from negative influences. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Dragon}}|/|{{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=Tatsu|1=Japanese dragon}}}}''' – A mythical creature that is seen as a symbol for great power, wisdom, leadership and success, and is said to bring strength, luck, and fortune. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Fraternity|en|Fraternity|qid=Q996839|lt=Fraternity|1=Fraternity}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Tei]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}*}}''' – A fountain near the entrance of a shrine or at a Buddhist temple where worshipers can cleanse their hands and mouths before worship. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heaven and earth}}||Tenchi}}''' – A term referring to the union of opposites. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly hall}}||Tendō}}''' – {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}, not unlike {{ill|Nirvana (Buddhism)|en|Nirvana (Buddhism)|qid=Q16256466|lt=nirvana|1=Nirvana (Buddhism)}}. Also known as ''Tengoku''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|qid=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}}}}''' – A type of legendary creature found in Japanese folk religion. They are considered a type of ''yōkai'' or ''kami''. The ''tengu'' were originally thought to take the forms of birds of prey, and they are traditionally depicted with both human and avian characteristics. * '''{{nihongo|||Tengu-kakushi}}''' – A term used to refer to the mysterious disappearance or death of a person that happens when an angered ''tengu'' takes a person away. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|sky god}}||{{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|qid=Q1753428|lt=Tenjin|1=Tenjin (kami)}}}}''' – The {{ill|Apotheosis|en|Apotheosis|qid=Q580995|lt=deification|1=Apotheosis}} of {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845–903). Originally worshiped as a {{ill|Weather|en|Weather|qid=Q11663|lt=Weather|1=Weather}} ''kami'', Tenjin later became a [[:en:Patron deity|Patron deity]] of {{ill|Academic|en|Academic|qid=Q20122683|lt=academics|1=Academic}}, {{ill|Scholarship|en|Scholarship|qid=Q188823|lt=Scholarship|1=Scholarship}}, {{ill|Learning|en|Learning|qid=Q133500|lt=Learning|1=Learning}} and {{ill|Education|en|Education|qid=Q8434|lt=Education|1=Education}}, and the {{ill|Intelligentsia|en|Intelligentsia|qid=Q381142|lt=Intelligentsia|1=Intelligentsia}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly crown}}||Tenkan}}''' – A design of golden-{{ill|Filigree|en|Filigree|qid=Q1001313|lt=Filigree|1=Filigree}} crown worn by Buddha and celestial beings, such as ''tennyo''. Also worn by imperial princesses in the Heian period. Now worn by ''miko'' during formal occasions such as festivals. * '''{{nihongo3|lit. a "Wrapping Raiment" or "Heavenly Raiment"||Tenne}}''' – Similar to the ''hagoromo''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly person}}||{{ill|Tennin|en|Tennin|qid=Q7700291|lt=Tennin|1=Tennin}}}}''' – Divine beings found in Shinto and Japanese Buddhism that are similar to Western {{ill|Angel|en|Angel|qid=Q235113|lt=Angel|1=Angel}}s, {{ill|Nymph|en|Nymph|qid=Q373916|lt=Nymph|1=Nymph}}s, or {{ill|Fairy|en|Fairy|qid=Q8028|lt=fairies|1=Fairy}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly woman}}||{{ill|Tennin|en|Tennin|qid=Q7700291|lt=Tennyo|1=Tennin}}}}''' – Female ''tennin''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Reincarnation|en|Reincarnation|qid=Q128593|lt=Tensei/tenshō|1=Reincarnation}}}}''' – Reincarnation. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|heavenly messenger}}||{{ill|Tennin|en|Tennin|qid=Q7700291|lt=Tenshi|1=Tennin}}}}''' – Messenger ''tennin''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Teru teru bōzu|en|Teru teru bōzu|qid=Q1052591|lt=Teru teru bōzu|1=Teru teru bōzu}}}}''' – A small traditional handmade doll made of white paper or cloth that Japanese farmers began hanging outside of their window by a string. This talisman is supposed to have magical powers to bring good weather and to stop or prevent a rainy day. * '''The {{ill|Tide jewels|en|Tide jewels|qid=Q7800836|lt=Tide jewels|1=Tide jewels}}''' – The ''Kanju'' and the ''Manju''; two magical gems that the sea ''kami'' Watatsumi or Ryūjin used to control the tides. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hell of Revival}}||[[Naraka#Hot Narakas|Tokatsu Jigoku]]}}''' – The first and shallowest level of ''Jigoku'', where sinners who have committed murder are sent. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Torii]]}}''' – The Shinto gate at the entrance of a sacred area, usually a shrine. Also serves as the symbol for Shinto. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|market of the rooster}}||[[Kasai Shrine#Tori no ichi|Tori-no-Ichi]]}}''' – A festival in November. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|qid=Q12012294|lt=Stone lantern|1=Stone lantern}}}}''' – A lantern at a shrine or Buddhist temple. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Annum Shelf|Annum Alter}}||Toshidana}}''' – An altar specifically used for the New-Year, to commemorate a ''toshigami'' (below). Offerings include round cakes made of pounded rice, bottles of ''sake'', persimmons, tangerines, etc. See also ''Kamidana'' (above). * '''[[Toshigami]]''' – The ''kami'' of the year cycle. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Toso|en|Toso|qid=Q3176373|lt=Toso|1=Toso}}}}''' – A {{ill|Spice|en|Spice|qid=Q42527|lt=Spice|1=Spice}}d medicinal ''sake'', traditionally drunk during New Year celebrations in Japan. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]}}''' – Any sword that is ten hand widths long. In Japanese mythology, numerous deities own a sword of this kind. * '''{{nihongo|||Tsuina}}''' – A type of ritual of exorcism, specifically one performed during a funeral, by a ''hōsōshi'' to keep away flesh-eating, corpse-stealing ''yōkai'' away from the body being buried. ''Tsuina'' shares its origins with ''Setsubun'' in ''[[:en:Nuo rituals|Nuo]]'' rituals from China. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|moon viewing}}||{{ill|Tsukimi|en|Tsukimi|qid=Q1050292|lt=Tsukimi|1=Tsukimi}}}}''' – A festival honouring the autumn moon. The celebration of the {{ill|Full moon|en|Full moon|qid=Q104641|lt=Full moon|1=Full moon}} typically takes place on the 15th day of the eighth month of the traditional [[Japanese calendar]]; the {{ill|Waxing moon|en|Waxing moon|qid=Q37887893|lt=Waxing moon|1=Waxing moon}} is celebrated on the 13th day of the ninth month. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Tsukubai|en|Tsukubai|qid=Q2389237|lt=Tsukubai|1=Tsukubai}}}}''' – A [[:en:Washbasin|Washbasin]] provided at the entrance to a holy place for visitors to purify themselves by the ritual washing of hands and rinsing of the mouth. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Tsukumogami]]}}''' – ''yōkai'' who came to being from tools that have acquired a ''kami'' or spirit. According to the ''Ise Monogatari Shō'', there is a theory originally from the ''Onmyōki'' (陰陽記) that foxes and tanuki, among other beings, that have lived for at a hundred years and changed forms are considered tsukumogami. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|moon reader}}| / |[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]]}}''' – The ''kami'' of the moon, the son of Izanagi, the younger brother of Amaterasu Ōkami, and the older brother of Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Spouses Structure}}| or |{{ill|Tsumairi|en|Tsumairi|qid=Q7883237|lt=Tsumairi|1=Tsumairi}}'' or ''tsumairi-zukuri}}''' – A style of construction in which the building has its main entrance on the side which runs perpendicular to the roof's ridge (gabled side). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|sin}}||[[Tsumi]]}}''' – A violation committed against legal, social moral, ethical, or religious rules. It is most often used in the religious and moral sense. See also ''amatsu tsumi'', ''kunitsu tsumi'', ''imi'', and ''kegare''. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|crane}}||{{ill|Crane (bird)|en|Crane (bird)|qid=Q25365|lt=Tsuru|1=Crane (bird)}}}}''' – A species of bird that is native to Japan and to Japanese culture. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Hanging Dolls}}|/|''Tsurushi-bina''}}''' – Strings of hanging dolls, als all manner of {{ill|en:Luck|en|en:Luck|qid=Q1970348|lt=auspicious|1=Luck}} symbols (cranes, etc.), that are a traditional decoration, like a [[:en:Hanging mobile|Hanging mobile]], for ''Hinamatsuri''. Also known as {{nihongo3|{{lit|"Hanging Thing"}}||''Sagemon''}}, {{nihongo3|{{lit|"Hanging Thing"}}||''Sagarimono''}}, {{nihongo3|{{lit|"Hanging Decoration/Wreath"}}||''Tsurushi kazari''}} and {{nihongo3|{{lit|"Umbrella [of] good luck"}}||''Kasafuku''}}
==U== * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|birthplace deity}}||Ubusunagami}}''' – A type of tutelary deity, a guardian god or spirit of a particular place in Shinto. The term ''ubusuna-mode'' (visiting one's own birth god) became widely used for visiting one's hometown and shrine. See also ''sore'' and ''ujigami''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Uchide no kozuchi|en|Uchide no kozuchi|qid=Q7876713|lt=Uchide no kozuchi|1=Uchide no kozuchi}}}}''' – Daikokuten's magic, lucky coin-stamping mallet. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|clan deity}}||[[Ujigami]]}}''' – A {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=guardian|1=Tutelary deity}} god or spirit of a particular place in Shinto. * '''{{nihongo|||Ukehi'' or ''Ukei}}''' – A Shinto {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}} ritual. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|sustainable food deity}}||{{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}}}''' – The ''kami'' of food. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|visit of the hour of the ox}}||[[Ushi no toki mairi]]}}''' – 1:00 am–3:00 am; the equivalent of the {{ill|Witching hour|en|Witching hour|qid=Q2711974|lt=Witching hour|1=Witching hour}}. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|existing body}}||Utsushimi}}''' – A term for a visible, physical form (of a deity). * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|existing world}}||Utsushiyo}}''' – A term for a visible, physical world.
==W== * '''{{nihongo|||Waraningyō}}''' – Depending-upon their intended use, ''Wara-Ningyō'' can be used either for cleansing a person of sins and impurities, casting spells to protect or empower, or inflict a curse upon another person via ritual impalement. Originating from Taoist rituals, they are nailed to trees to curse people, during ''ushi no toki mairi''.
== Y == * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|misfortune}}||Yaku}}''' – The bad luck that one accumulates every day. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|year of misfortune}}||Yakudoshi}}''' – A period of time where a person experiences their own "age of calamity", where they will suffer misfortunes. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Yama|en|Yama|qid=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}}}}''' – Another name for {{nihongo3|{{lit|Evil Gates King}}||{{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|qid=Q2089602|lt=Enma-Ō|1=Yama (Buddhism)}}}}, the ''kami'' who oversees ''[[Naraka|Naraku]]''. * '''{{nihongo|||[[Yamabushi]]}}''' – A practitioner of ''shugendō''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Yamajijii|en|Yamajijii|qid=Q11470023|lt=Yamajijii|1=Yamajijii}}}}''' – A male ''yōkai'' found in Japanese folklore and Noh. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|mountain crone}}||{{ill|Yama-uba|en|Yama-uba|qid=Q1056461|lt=Yama-uba|1=Yama-uba}}}}''' – A female ''yōkai'' found in Japanese folklore and Noh, and is most often described as a monstrous crone. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Mountain Child}}||{{ill|Yamawaro|en|Yamawaro|qid=Q10836901|lt=Yamawaro|1=Yamawaro}}}}''' – A childlike ''yōkai'' found in Japanese folklore and Noh. * '''''Yaoyorozu no kami''''' (, {{lit|eight million ''kami''}}) – All ''kami''. * '''''[[Yata no Kagami]]''''' () – A sacred mirror that is part of the Imperial Regalia of Japan. It is said to be housed in Ise Grand Shrine in Mie Prefecture, Japan. The ''Yata no Kagami'' represents wisdom or honesty, depending on the source. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|spear}}||{{ill|Yari|en|Yari|qid=Q749087|lt=Yari|1=Yari}}}}''' – A spear, sometimes dedicated to shrines (along with other weapons, such as ''Katana'' and ''Naginata''), and indicative of a ''kami''{{'s}} power to ward-off negative influences. * '''{{nihongo|||Yashikigami}}''' – A ''kami'' of a specific plot of land, house, household, etc. * '''{{nihongo|||Yashiro}}''' – A generic term for ''shrine'', similar to ''jinja''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|qid=Q503186|lt=Yōkai|1=Yōkai}}}}''' – An umbrella term that covers many supernatural beings, ''Yōkai'' can also be used when referring to humans, animals, objects, and even ''kami''. * '''{{nihongo|||Yōma}}''' – A type of ghost, phantom, apparition, monster, or demon. * '''{{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}''' ({{lang|ja|}}, {{lit|Underworld}}) – The land of the dead; the afterworld, the underworld: according to Shinto mythology, as related in ''Kojiki'', it is where the dead go in the afterlife. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}}}''' – An object capable of attracting ''kami'' for a ceremony. Trees, rocks, ''magatama'', ''gohei'', and people can be ''yorishiro''. * '''{{nihongo|||Yorimashi}}''' – A human ''yorishiro'', in particular a child or woman, used by a faith healer for oracles. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|armour}}||Yoroi}}''' – A suit of armour, sometimes dedicated to shrines, and indicative of a ''kami''{{'s}} power to protect from negative influences. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|{{ill|Courage|en|Courage|qid=Q208160|lt=Courage|1=Courage}}}}||[[Bushido#Eight virtues of bushido (as defined by Nitobe Inazō)|Yū]]}}''' – One of the virtues of ''bushido''. * '''{{nihongo|||Yūki}}''' – A type of demonic ghost or phantom; a demonic {{ill|Poltergeist|en|Poltergeist|qid=Q216893|lt=Poltergeist|1=Poltergeist}}. An ''[[onryō]]'' is a kind of ''yūki''. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Yūrei|en|Yūrei|qid=Q11050647|lt=Yūrei|1=Yūrei}}}}''' – A type of {{ill|Ghost|en|Ghost|qid=Q45529|lt=Ghost|1=Ghost}} or phantom. An ''[[onryō]]'' is a kind of ''yūrei''.
==Z== * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Zōni|en|Zōni|qid=Q249169|lt=Zōni|1=Zōni}}}}''' – ''A'' Japanese soup containing ''mochi'' rice cakes; associated with the Japanese New Year and its tradition of ''Osechi'' ceremonial foods. * '''{{nihongo|||{{ill|Zuijin|en|Zuijin|qid=Q116146|lt=Zuijin|1=Zuijin}}}}''' – ''Kami'' warrior-guardian figures. They are often depicted to be holding bows and arrows, or wearing three silver rings. The name was originally applied to the bodyguards of the Emperor of Japan. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|architecture}}||{{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|qid=Q3080012|lt=Zukuri|1=Shinto architecture}}}}''' – A style of construction a building, that a shrine has usually been built in.
